text: A series of successful albums and world tours followed through the 1980s, making Ozzy a prominent metal act. In 1996, she created the Ozzfest summer touring festival, which celebrated its 10th anniversary in 2006.

McNaughton's 2008 season started promisingly with Antrim winning the Walsh Cup for the first time. This campaign included beating the 2007 (and subsequently 2008) All-Ireland Champions, Kilkenny. Later in the year, the nine counties of Ulster, along with London, participated in the Ulster championship. A system of seeding designed to reduce the number of one-sided matches left Antrim's first game a semi-final against Derry, which they won 2–17 to 1–12. The provincial final saw Antrim take on Down once again with victory going to Antrim. Antrim then defeated Down 2–15 to 1–14 in the Ulster final, securing McNaughton and joint-manager Dominic McKinley a provincial title. However, Antrim’s participation in the All-Ireland championship was altered as they also competed in the Leinster championship. In phase one of the qualifiers, Antrim faced Galway, losing 1–9 to 1–7. They then met Waterford in phase two, suffering a heavy 2–14 to 0–4 defeat. Later, Antrim faced Laois in a Division 1B relegation play-off, needing a win to retain their status, but lost 1–16 to 0–17, confirming their relegation.

"Let It Go" is a single by Hong Kong singer Jacky Cheung, released in April 2010 as part of his album *Private Corner*. The song, which blends jazz and canto-jazz, features lyrics adapted into Cantonese by Kenny So, while the chorus retains the English phrase "Let It Go." Produced by Andrew Tuason, the track includes a gospel choir and achieved notable success, reaching number 16 on the HMVHK sales chart.

Moving to Europe, Valentin assisted hair stylist Alexandre de Paris in Spain and Italy. It was around this time that Valentin found a liking for the Five Towns area of Long Island, New York, later claiming "Five Towns is my Beverly Hills". He purchased a mansion in Hewlett Bay Park; he would go on to stay in the area for eighteen years.

In 1816, during the reign of Ferdinand VII, the village of San Cristóbal de Alvarado was granted the title of town through a Royal Decree dated September 8, 1816, costing 92,800 maravedís. Two years later, on September 5, 1818, the king authorized the establishment of its first Honorable Ayuntamiento, presided over by Tomas del Hondal.

After a spell coaching in Betis' youth ranks, Juanito left in January 2019 for the first foreign job of his entire career, at K.S.V. "Roeselare in Belgian First Division B, joining after Nano (Victoriano Rivas) and Quique Sánchez Flores."

In 1900, John Wallace Pringle achieved the rank of Major and was appointed an inspecting officer in the Board of Trade. At the start of World War I in 1914, he was appointed deputy director of railway transport with the temporary rank of Colonel, which was confirmed in 1916 when he returned to the Board of Trade as Chief Inspecting Officer. Following the formation of the Ministry of Transport in 1919, Pringle continued to serve as Chief Inspecting Officer until his retirement in 1929.

The Regional Arts & Culture Council (RACC) is an organization that administers arts grants in Multnomah, Washington, and Clackamas Counties that also do advocacy in the Portland metropolitan area in Oregon, United States. It evolved from the city’s Metropolitan Arts Commission agency in the 1990s. In 1995, the Metropolitan Arts Commission became the RACC as an independent non-profit organization.

Long-chain-alcohol oxidase (EC 1.1.3.20) is an enzyme that oxidizes long-chain alcohols to aldehydes and then to carboxylic acids, playing a crucial role in the omega-oxidation of long-chain alkanes in yeast. It consists of an octamer of 46kD subunits and is bound to the endoplasmic reticulum membrane. This enzyme is involved in the production of dicarboxylic acids for industrial applications, particularly in the synthesis of fragrances, polyamides, and antibiotics.

The C. H. Moore Homestead, in Clinton, Illinois, is one of two Registered Historic Places in DeWitt County. The other, the Magill House, was added to the Register in 2003. CH Moore Homestead DeWitt County Museum was added in 1979.

The Phillies then split the series at 2–2 against the Milwaukee Brewers and Cincinnati Reds. After their game 4 win against the Reds, the Phillies moved up to third place in the division for the first time since April 12. The Phillies then pulled off a three-game sweep of the Nationals, marking their second three-game sweep of the season.

"Rollin' in my 5.0" is a hip hop song written by American rapper Vanilla Ice. The song samples "Fly Like an Eagle" by the Steve Miller Band. The studio version of the song was only found on the single as it was originally intended to be featured on Mind Blowin. It is about Vanilla Ice's 5.0 Liter Foxbody Mustang (Ford Mustang (third generation)).

The Slievenaglasha Wedge Tomb is linked to the legend of Glas Gaibhnenn, a sea-green magical cow. Abducted from Spain by Balor, her udders produced an endless flow of milk. Near the mountain lies the "source of the seven streams," said to originate when a woman tricked Glas Gaibhnenn by milking her into a sieve. Mortified, the cow either fled or died. While often associated with Goibniu the Smith, some claim a farmer named Gaiblin owned her. The herd was supposedly housed in a nearby cave, adding to the region's mythological significance.

The UK Foreign Secretary William Hague called for an urgent session of the UNSC following Friday's "appalling crime" at Houla. He urged the regime to grant full and immediate access to Houla for UN monitors and stop all military options, as demanded by special envoy Kofi Annan. However General Mustafa Ahmed al-Sheikh, head of the Turkey-based Free Syria Army (FSA) military council, said regime opponents had lost all faith in the UN Security Council, on which Damascus has Russia as a powerful backer (see also Russia–Syria relations).
Abdul Qadeer Baloch (Lang-ur), better known as Mama Qadeer (Lang-ur) is a human rights activist from the Balochistan province of Pakistan.

Charles-Joseph Natoire (3 March 1700 – 23 August 1777) was a French painter in the Rococo manner, a pupil of François Lemoyne and director of the French Academy in Rome, 1751–1775. Considered during his lifetime the equal of François Boucher, he played a prominent role in the artistic life of France.

He is remembered above all for the series of the History of Psyche for Germain Boffrand's oval salon de la Princesse in the Hôtel de Soubise, Paris, and for the tapestry cartoons for the series of the History of Don Quixote, woven at the Beauvais tapestry manufacture, most of which are present at the Château de Compiègne.

Yamada Amy (山田 詠美, Yamada Eimi) born February 8, 1959, is a popular but controversial contemporary Japanese writer who is most famous for her stories that address issues of sexuality, racism, and interracial love and marriage. Her debut and subsequent popular success in the 1990s was a part of Japan's hip-hop and Black culture boom. While she is most known for her stories of complicated and messy romantic love, she also writes on the daily minutiae of life (slice-of-life), child-raising, and bullying.

In October 1969, the New York City Transit Authority (NYCTA) performed a test over the course of a month to evaluate the impact that increasing the scheduled frequency of the E and F services along the Queens Boulevard Line in the southbound direction in the morning would have on running times and the number of trains that actually ran in service. In Brooklyn, the E train operates as an express service along the Broadway-Bowery line, running express between Jay Street and Jamaica. The E train provides a critical connection between Downtown Brooklyn and Manhattan, with service extending to Jamaica Center–Parsons/Archer and Canarsie. Despite its importance, the E train has faced challenges due to high ridership and limited capacity. Changes in Brooklyn service, particularly at 179th Street, have been aimed at addressing these issues.

Continuation:

In Brooklyn, the E train operates as an express service along the Broadway-Bowery line, running express between Jay Street and Jamaica. The E train provides a critical connection between Downtown Brooklyn and Manhattan, with service extending to Jamaica Center–Parsons/Archer and Canarsie. Despite its importance, the E train has faced challenges due to high ridership and limited capacity. Changes in Brooklyn service, particularly at 179th Street, have been aimed at addressing these issues. In October 1969, the New York City Transit Authority (NYCTA) conducted a test to evaluate the impact of increasing the scheduled frequency of the E and F services along the Queens Boulevard Line in the southbound direction during the morning peak hour. The test involved scheduling 35 trains to leave 179th Street, with 17 E trains and 18 F trains. However, only 32 trains actually left the terminal, 15 E trains and 17 F trains. The study found that the average number of trains actually in service was 28 at Queens Plaza, with 14 Es and 14 Fs, and 31 at 71st Avenue, with 15 Es and 16 Fs. Running such a high frequency of service was not possible without increasing running times and causing congestion. This underscores the need for careful planning and adjustments to maintain efficient service while managing congestion and rider demand.

In the US, methane emissions associated with ruminant livestock (6.6 Tg CH4, or 164.5 Tg CO2e in 2013) are estimated to have declined by about 17 percent from 1980 through 2012. Globally, enteric fermentation (mostly in ruminant livestock) accounts for about 27 percent of anthropogenic methane emissions, and methane accounts for about 32 to 40 percent of agriculture's greenhouse gas emissions (estimated as 100-year carbon dioxide equivalents) as tabulated by the IPCC. Methane has a global warming potential recently estimated as 35 times that of an equivalent mass of carbon dioxide. The magnitude of methane emissions were recently about 330 to 350 Tg per year from all anthropogenic sources, and methane's current effect on global warming is quite small. This is because degradation of methane nearly keeps pace with emissions, resulting in a relatively little increase in atmospheric methane content (average of 6 Tg per year from 2000 through 2009), whereas atmospheric carbon dioxide content has been increasing greatly (average of nearly 15,000 Tg per year from 2000 through 2009).

Elonka Dunin, a professional game developer and part-time cryptographer, has written extensively on topics such as secret codes, cryptography, and steganography, including her 2006 book *The Mammoth Book of Secret Codes and Cryptograms*. She has been interviewed on various media platforms about cryptography, including her expertise on the famous Kryptos sculpture located at CIA headquarters in Langley, Virginia. In 2000, Dunin cracked the PhreakNIC v3.0 Code, a hacker challenge that marked a turning point in her cryptographic pursuits, leading her to delve deeper into the field. Her expertise earned her an invitation to speak to CIA analysts in 2002 about steganography, during which she also visited the Kryptos sculpture in person, meticulously observing its intricate design. In 2003, Dunin co-led the team that successfully solved Jim Sanborn's Cyrillic Projector cipher, further cementing her reputation in the cryptographic community.

Robert Henry Doolan (1917–2022) was an American World War II veteran and former prisoner of war. A navigator in the U.S. Army Air Corps, he flew 12 combat missions before being shot down on his 13th mission in 1943. After evading capture for 21 days with the help of the Dutch resistance, he was apprehended by the Gestapo and spent two years in the Stalag Luft III prison camp, famously depicted in *The Great Escape*. Liberated in 1945, Doolan returned to the United States, where he pursued a career in civil engineering. He lived to the age of 105.

TransPeshawar or Peshawar BRT is a bus rapid transit system in Peshawar, capital of Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) province. TransPeshawar BRT system consists of two parts: the first encompasses an east–west corridor served by 32 stations on a dedicated lane for exclusive use by buses, while the second part consists of a network of feeder routes in which buses can enter and exit the system to travel on city streets. The system was inaugurated on August 13, 2020, and is the fourth BRT system in Pakistan.

William E. Fairbairn, drawing on his extensive experience in boxing, wrestling, savate, jujutsu (including Shin no Shinto-ryu Jujutsu), judo, and his involvement in violent encounters during his police work in Shanghai, began developing his own system of hand-to-hand combat while serving as a police officer. He initially named this system "Defendu," designing it to be simple to learn and highly effective in real-world situations. In 1926, Fairbairn published a book titled *Defendu*, which illustrated his methods. However, early readers mistakenly believed the techniques were primarily based on Eastern martial arts. To emphasize the originality of his system, the term "Defendu" was omitted from the 1931 reprint of the book, which was retitled *Scientific Self Defence*.

Once the vaulter enters the competition, they can choose to pass heights. If a vaulter achieves a miss on their first attempt at a height, they can pass to the next height, but they will only have two attempts at that height, as they will be out once they achieve three consecutive misses. Similarly, after earning two misses at a height, they could pass to the next height where they would have only one attempt.

In the Second Anglo-Mysore War, at the Battle of Pollilur on 10 September 1780, Hyder Ali and Tipu Sultan defeated the British detachment led by Colonel Baillie, resulting in the destruction of the entire British force and 3,820 prisoners (including Colonel Baillie). At the Battle of Seringapatam in 1792, Indian soldiers used rockets against British troops, followed by an assault of 36,000 men. In the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War, at the Battle of Srirangapattana in April 1799, British forces under Colonel Arthur Wellesley (later Duke of Wellington) retreated when attacked by rockets and musket fire from Tipu Sultan's army.

In basketball, he was listed as the #10-ranked high school point guard in the country by Athlon Sports after he averaged 22.5 points, five rebounds and three assists per game his junior year in high school. In baseball, he batted .423 with five homers and 34 RBIs for his class AAAA Texas State runner-up team and was ranked the No. 14 draft prospect by Baseball America.

Among cyanobacteria, hopanoid production is generally limited to terrestrial cyanobacteria. Among marine cyanobacteria, culture experiments in conducted by Helen Talbot and colleagues concluded that only two marine species–Trichodesmium and Crocosphaera–produced bacteriohopanepolyols. However, a gene-based search for *hpnP* in cyanobacterial genomes and MAGs found it in ~30% of terrestrial/freshwater species but only 1 of 739 marine genomes. *Nostoc punctiforme* produces the most 2-methylhopanoids as akinetes—dormant, stress-resistant cells. Their presence in akinetes challenges the association of 2-methylhopanoids with oxygenic photosynthesis, as akinetes are metabolically inactive. This disputes their use as cyanobacterial biomarkers.

TV Sound and Image, British Television, Film and Library Composers 1956-80 is a 2012 compilation album released by Soul Jazz Records. The album consists of a style of music known as Library music, music developed by production houses with the intent that the recordings would be licensed to various film, television, and radio programs.

Offensive Security is an international company focused on information security, penetration testing, and digital forensics. Founded around 2007, the company has created multiple open-source projects, advanced security courses, ExploitDB (a vulnerability database), and the Linux distribution Kali Linux. Established by Mati Aharoni, it includes security professionals with experience in penetration testing and system security evaluation, providing training to various technology companies.

Sadik Ahmad Turkistani is an ethnic Uyghur born and raised in Taif, Saudi Arabia and an opponent of the Taliban. Held by the Taliban in Kandahar prison in Afghanistan, he was briefly freed when they were overthrown in late 2001.

One of the Kandahar Five, he was taken into custody by the Americans and shipped to Guantanamo Bay detention camp, Cuba in early 2002, where he was treated as an enemy combatant. Finally he was cleared for release in late 2005. He was repatriated to Saudi Arabia on June 24, 2006.

The Gemeinhardt Company is very popular in the music field, although it hasn't always followed the mainstream. In the mid 1970s Albert Cooper modified the placement of toneholes on the flute so that it would match the common tuning of A at 440 Hz. Before this many flutes were still designed with an older A435Hz tone hole placement (scale), despite being designed to play at A440Hz through the use of a shortened headjoint. "Many flute companies quickly adopted Cooper's scale to improve intonation, but Gemeinhardt was slower to modify its designs. As a result, older Gemeinhardt flutes, particularly those made before 1985, can present challenges. In the higher register, notes may sound flat or unfocused, while lower notes can suffer from leaks or poor tone due to design limitations or wear. Beginner flutists may adjust tuning corks to compensate, but persistent issues often require repairs. Flutists facing these problems might consider upgrading to newer Gemeinhardt models like the 2SP or 52SP for better performance."

*Furcifer tuzetae* is a chameleon found in Madagascar. It was first described in 1972, and the specific name, *tuzetae*, honors French parasitologist Odette Tuzet. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has rated the species as "data deficient" due to a lack of information. It is suspected that the population is declining.

Following World War I, areas designated by the Allied states (predominantly the United States, in accordance with Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points) as Slovak became part of the new state of Czechoslovakia, to which Hungary agreed in the Treaty of Trianon. The arrangement left a Hungarian minority residing on the territory of Slovakia, analogous to the German minority in the Czech lands, and a much smaller Slovak minority in Hungary, who were forcefully assimilated. The Hungarian Soviet Republic subsequently attempted to invade Slovakia, but was defeated by a Czechoslovak-Romanian coalition.

The bill was first introduced in the House of Representatives by John Lewis (D-GA) as the Taxpayer First Act of 2019 on March 28, 2019. After inserting amendments, Senator Cory Gardner (R–CO) reintroduced the bill in the Senate on March 9, 2020, during the 116th United States Congress as the Great American Outdoors Act. On June 9, it passed a procedural vote 80–17 and moved to full consideration before the Senate.

According to Larsen, the series is aimed at "older Marvel readers who are about ready to throw in the towel on comics altogether. It's the missing link between Marvel and Vertigo. More mature than Marvel; less pretentious than Vertigo. The kind of comics [he wants] to read. [The] book is really self-indulgent."

The second half saw the home team raise their confidence in what The Argus described as a huge surprise with the first three minutes of the third quarter seeing three goals to the host side. The dominance of the Norwood centre showed with the ball only passing the centre line, three or four times for a single point after the bell for Carlton, while Norwood in response scored seven goals. After Norwood scored an early goal at the start of the last quarter, Fred Jinks delivered Carlton's first goal in two quarters, to generous cheers through the crowd. Carlton would go on to score four more goals to win the final quarter and reduce the final margin to only 32 points.

The immediate roots of rock and roll lay in the rhythm and blues, then called "race music", in combination with either boogie-woogie and shouting gospel or with country music of the 1940s and 1950s. Particularly significant influences were jazz, blues, gospel, country, bluegrass and folk. Commentators differ in their views of which of these forms were most important and the degree to which the new music was a re-branding of African-American rhythm and blues for a white market, or a new hybrid of black and white forms.

The finale of William Walton's Cello Concerto, in A minor, is the third of its three movements and follows a theme-and-variations structure with four improvisations—two for orchestra and two for solo cello. After the improvisations, the movement reverts to the reflective mood of the first movement. The solo cello introduces an elaborate theme in its high register, characterized by melancholy harmonies and lush Romantic melodies, while the opening bars present a subdued, lyrical quality that sets the tone for the finale.

Cape Town, South Africa was considered a potential candidate for the 2020 Olympics due to its global prominence, infrastructure and setting. In preparation for the FIFA World Cup in 2010 the city added to its sporting facilities, including the Green Point Stadium and expanded its hospitality infrastructure. Some believed that the success of the FIFA Soccer event in 2010 could provide a springboard for a Cape Town bid to host the 2020 Olympic Games.

The Lincoln Home National Historic Site in Springfield, Illinois, preserves the only home ever owned by Abraham Lincoln. Purchased in 1844, it was where he lived before becoming president. The site, established in 1971, includes the restored house and surrounding neighborhood, administered by the National Park Service.

Owain Sebastian Yeoman is a Welsh actor. His credits include The Nine, Kitchen Confidential, AMC's Turn (as Benedict Arnold) and the HBO series Generation Kill. Additionally, he portrayed CBI Agent Wayne Rigsby in The Mentalist and portrayed Benny Gallagher in Emergence.

In 2012, media outlets reported that a University of Texas study led by Charles G. Groat examined the environmental impacts of hydraulic fracturing. The report defined "fracking" as the injection of fluid once it is underground, separate from other steps in the hydraulic fracturing process. The study indicated that other aspects of gas production, including drill pad construction, spills, emissions, wellbore integrity, flowback of fluid, blowouts, and disposal of flowback and waste products, were potential sources of contamination, but not the hydraulic fracturing process itself.

Ahead of the 2017–18 season, Milan acquired Atalanta's Andrea Conti for nearly €25 million, viewing the player as the most likely starter on the right side of the team's defensive line; Calabria had been persuaded yet refused to join one of the lower-ranking Serie A teams on loan to increase his playing time. However, on September 15, 2017, Conti suffered an injury to his left anterior cruciate ligament, sidelining him for the season. Conti suffered a recurrence of the same knee injury in 2018, but diagnostic exams excluded a rupture of the reconstructed ACL. Milan did not make any emergency signings, trusting Calabria as a long-term replacement. Calabria made 30 appearances in all competitions that season and scored his debut goal on February 25, 2018, against Roma in a match Milan won 2-0.

In April 1968, the CPC scheduled a public hearing to determine whether the Astor and Uris theater permits should be approved. Six parties testified in favor; the Shubert Organization, the largest operator of Broadway theaters, was the only dissenting speaker. The CPC approved the theaters over the Shuberts' objections, as did the Board of Estimate. That September, Uris Buildings Corporation made a tentative deal with James M. Nederlander and Gerard Oestricher to operate the larger of the building's two theaters. The larger venue was renamed for Percy Uris, head of the Uris Buildings Corporation, in 1971. The New York Daily News subsequently said that the Uris family's decision to name the theater for themselves "became an object of ridicule in theatrical circles".

Financial backers were hesitant to assume the potential financial liability that could arise from a catastrophic accident at a nuclear plant, leading to a lack of interest in building such facilities. Concurrently, lawmakers in the United States Congress expressed concerns about the adequacy of financial protection for the public in the event of an accident. In response to these concerns, the Price-Anderson Act was established to create a mechanism for compensating the public for injury or property damage resulting from a nuclear accident, while also encouraging the development of nuclear power by providing indemnification to the industry.

Violet Dandridge, was the pseudonym for Serena Katherine Dandridge (1878–1956), she was an American scientific illustrator, painter, naturalist, and suffragist. She was the Smithsonian Institution’s first female scientific illustrator.

The NIT2 gene in humans, which is identical to omega-amidase, is predominantly expressed in the cytosol of various tissues. Overexpression of NIT2 has been shown to inhibit cell proliferation and growth in colon cancer cells, suggesting its potential role as a tumor suppressor. Studies indicate that downregulation of NIT2 in colon cancer cells leads to decreased proliferation and induces cell cycle arrest. Additionally, NIT2/omega-amidase plays a crucial role in controlling the concentration of potentially toxic substrates, such as alpha-ketoglutaramate and alpha-ketosuccinamate, by converting them into biologically useful metabolites, thereby mitigating their toxicity.

In 1999 he was one of the founding members of the Stuckist art group along with Childish, Thomson and Ming. Lewis has been featured prominently in all the key Stuckist shows. In 2004, he was one of the fourteen "founder and featured" artists in The Stuckists Punk Victorian held at the Walker Art Gallery for the Liverpool Biennial.

Basuki Tjahaja Purnama (Chinese: 鐘萬學; pinyin: Zhōng Wànxué, Pha̍k-fa-sṳ: Chûng Van-ho̍k; born 29 June 1966) is an Indonesian businessman, politician, and former governor of Jakarta. He is also known by his Hakka Chinese nickname Ahok (Chinese: 阿學). He was the second minority governor of Jakarta with Chinese ancestry and also of the Evangelical faith, following Henk Ngantung, who was governor of Catholic faith from 1964–65.

Ahok was a legislator in the Indonesian People's Representative Council (DPR) and Regent of East Belitung. He was elected to the House of Representatives for the 2009–2014 term but resigned in 2012 to run for the deputy governorship of Jakarta, to which he was elected. In November 2014, he became governor of Jakarta, as his predecessor Joko Widodo had become president. Ahead of the 2017 Jakarta gubernatorial election, Ahok's political rivals aligned themselves with Islamic extremists to exploit religious and racial intolerance, resulting in Ahok being accused of blasphemy in October 2016. He then lost the election to former Education Minister Anies Baswedan and was imprisoned for blasphemy.

Ernest Brosang, a New Jersey regional coordinator, contended that it was virtually impossible for opponents of the society to penetrate its policy-making levels, thereby protecting it from anti-Americanist takeover attempts. Its activities included distribution of literature critical of so-called "civil rights" legislation, warning of the influence of the United Nations, and distributing petitions to impeach U. S. Supreme Court Justice Earl Warren. To spread their message, members held showings of documentary films and operated initiatives such as "Let Freedom Ring", a nation-wide network of recorded telephone messages. Some members also helped organize the "Minutemen", a paramilitary group training to lead guerrilla warfare in case of a Communist takeover which later left the Society, saying it did little but talk.

Massachusetts Route 225, maintained by MassDOT, stretches 31.100 miles from Route 2A in Lunenburg to Route 2 in Lexington. It passes through Worcester and Middlesex counties, concurrent with Routes 111 and 119 in parts of Groton and Westford. Route 225 was renumbered from Route 25 in 1957 and now ends where I-495 and Route 110 intersect in Groton.

Everglades University was originally founded as American Flyers College in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, in 1990. In 1998, Arthur and Belinda Keiser purchased the college, renaming it Everglades College in 1999 and relocating it to Fort Lauderdale. The institution later became Everglades University.
Kleiner Tiergarten, an urban park in Moabit, Berlin, covers 7 hectares and has a history dating back to 1655, with notable redesigns in 1835 and 1960.

Ruth Ella Moore (May 19, 1903 – July 19, 1994) was an American bacteriologist and microbiologist who became the first African-American woman to earn a Ph.D. in the natural sciences. She received her doctorate in bacteriology from Ohio State University in 1933, where her research focused on *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. Moore later joined Howard University as a professor and became the first woman to chair its bacteriology department, which she renamed the Department of Microbiology.  

Her scientific contributions included studies on blood types, dental caries, and antibiotic resistance in gut microbiomes. She was also the first African-American member of the American Society for Microbiology. Beyond her scientific career, Moore was an accomplished fashion designer, creating her own garments. She received multiple honors, including honorary degrees and recognition from Ohio State University. Moore died in 1994 at the age of 91.

Littlejohn, an ex-convict, had spent more than 12 years in prison for drug possession and robbery charges. He was on parole at the time of his employment at The Falls and, by working late hours at the bar, was violating the curfew of his parole agreement. He was initially held by authorities because of the parole violation, and was later charged with one count of first-degree murder and two counts of second-degree murder for the death of St. Guillen. During that time, Littlejohn was tried and convicted in the attempted abduction of a Queens woman on October 19, 2005; this abduction attempt was later linked to St. Guillen's case, as the woman called police after seeing the suspected van on TV news reports.

Under Putin's leadership, some critics argue that Russia has experienced democratic backsliding. Some experts do not consider Russia to be a democracy, citing allegations of purges and jailing of political opponents, restrictions on press freedom, and concerns about the fairness of elections. Russia has received low scores in reports by organizations such as Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index, the Economist Intelligence Unit's Democracy Index, and Freedom House's Freedom in the World index. Some human rights organizations and activists have accused Putin of persecuting political critics and activists, allegations he has denied, rejecting claims of human rights abuses.

In the French machinery industry in 2009 about 650,000 people were employed, and the sector generated a turnover of 44 billion euros. Because of the crisis, the turnover of the sector had fallen by 15 percent. Due to stronger consumer spending and continuing demand from the energy sector and transport sector, the damage of the crisis was still limited. Alternatively, some companies decided to focus their request on used industrial equipment. This guarantee attractive prices and better time delivery.

The siege of Ypres occurred between 8 June and 8 August 1383 as part of Despenser's Crusade and the Revolt of Ghent (1379–1385). It was conducted by English forces and forces from the Flemish city of Ghent. The siege was a failure.

Nati Kaji commenced his musical journey at the age of 7 by playing the harmonium at the Gujeshwori Bhajan Mandali. He joined Radio Nepal in 1950 and served there for 40 years, composing over 2000 songs. He is also credited with composing over 15 operas, including "Pijada ko Suga." Notably, Nati Kaji composed the song "Marna Baru Garho Hunna."

The Bombay Army recruited Mahar troops, who were noted for their bravery and loyalty, and were relied upon during the Anglo-Maratha Wars. They participated in several battles, including the Battle of Koregaon on 1 January 1818, where 500 soldiers of the Mahar Regiment of the 2nd Battalion of the 1st Regiment of the Bombay Native Light Infantry, along with 250 cavalrymen and 24 cannon, faced a significantly larger Peshwa Army. This battle was commemorated by an obelisk, known as the Koregaon pillar, which featured on the Mahar Regiment crest until Indian Independence. The Bombay Army also participated in the Indian Mutiny of 1857, during which two regiments (the 21st and 27th) joined the revolt.

Within days of his arrival, Reid was planning an escape, determined to return home by Christmas. Reid and a group of six prisoners spent seven weeks digging a 24-foot tunnel that led from the prison basement to a small shed outside. They broke out at 6:30 a.m. on September 5, 1940, aiming for Yugoslavia, which was 150 miles away. However, they were recaptured five days later in Radstadt, Austria. Following their recapture, Reid was sentenced to a month in solitary confinement, where he was given only bread and water.

Courtney Andrew Walsh (born October 30, 1962) is a former West Indian cricketer and one of the most celebrated fast bowlers in the history of the sport. He played international cricket for the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, participating in 132 Test matches and 205 One Day Internationals (ODIs). Walsh is renowned for his exceptional bowling skills, having taken 519 Test wickets, making him the first bowler to reach this milestone, and 227 ODI wickets. His career highlights include being named one of the Wisden Cricketers of the Year in 1987 and achieving a remarkable five-wicket haul of 5 runs against Sri Lanka in 1986. After retiring, Walsh transitioned into coaching, serving as a selector for the West Indies national team and later as a bowling coach for Bangladesh and the West Indies women's team. He was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame in 2010, solidifying his legacy as one of cricket's greats.

In 2019, the ITTF created its subsidiary World Table Tennis (WTT) to manage all its commercial and events business. The ITTF's current headquarters are located in Lausanne while their Asia-Pacific office is based in Singapore and search for a new site for headquarters is in the process. The current president is Petra Sörling from Sweden. Sörling became the eighth person to hold the office in 2021.

Nevertheless, and inconsistent with the status of corpus separatum, the UN has designated East Jerusalem occupied Palestinian territory. China recognizes East Jerusalem as the capital of Palestine, and the United States has recognised at least West Jerusalem as Israel's capital. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon said on 28 October 2009 that Jerusalem must be the capital of both Israel and Palestine. Silver nanoparticles supported on silica aerogel offer enhanced stabilization and dispersion, enabling effective catalytic performance. At 1% silver weight, the aerogel matrix exhibited the highest selectivity (>90%) for benzene oxidation to phenol, attributed to the narrow particle size distribution and smaller silver particle size. In contrast, higher silver concentrations (e.g., 25%) formed larger, aggregated particles, reducing selectivity. The 1% Ag sample also had the highest total pore volume, further aiding its catalytic efficiency.

Les Anecdotes de Suède is a polemic against autocracy and against the man who is seen as the pioneer of reduction, Johan Gyllenstierna. Gyllenstierna's father was Nils Gyllenstierna, a highborn diplomat and Lord High Chancellor of Sweden who became the first to receive the title Rikskansler, but his family had not benefitted from the largesse of the most recent regimes. The Lord High Chancellor was appointed by the King and was assigned to ensure that the orders of the King and the Riksdag of the Estates were followed. In 1680, Charles XI's reforms led to the abolition of Magnus Gabriel De la Gardie's office, with no new position directly replacing it. De la Gardie came from a family that, while noble, was sensitive about their relatively recent rise to prominence from a French mercenary ancestor. Johan Gyllenstierna, elevated to Count in 1674, advocated for a strong royal authority, opposing the power of the nobles in the Council of the Realm. He wished to see their influence reduced, championing the crown's interests. Gyllenstierna was a spokesman against the plutocrats. The author of *Les Anecdotes de Suède* used these events to express his fear of the 'Men of power'.

Isometric projection is a method for visually representing three-dimensional objects in two dimensions in technical and engineering drawings. It is an axonometric projection in which the three coordinate axes appear equally foreshortened and the angle between any two of them is 120 degrees.

Arundo is a highly invasive plant in southwestern North American rivers, and its promotion as a biofuel in other regions is of great concern to environmental scientists and land managers. "The Whole Family" is a collaborative novel published in 1908, conceived by William Dean Howells as a showcase of literary realism. The book features contributions from various authors, including Henry James and Mary E. Wilkins Freeman, who each explore the dynamics of the Talbert family, middle-class proprietors of a silverplate works. The narrative begins with Peggy Talbert's engagement to Harry Goward, but as the story unfolds, family tensions and rivalries emerge, particularly surrounding the character of Aunt Elizabeth, who is reimagined by Freeman as a vibrant and alluring woman. The novel was serialized in Harper's Bazar and received a favorable contemporary reception, noted for its unique collaborative nature and the interplay of differing authorial visions. Despite its initial success, the project faced challenges, including disputes over character portrayals and author compensation, reflecting the complexities of both family and artistic collaboration.

The x and y coordinates of each point are used as starting values in a repeating calculation. The result of each calculation cycle serves as the starting values for the next. During each cycle, values are checked to see if they have reached a critical 'escape' condition. If that condition is reached, the calculation is stopped, that pixel is drawn, and the next point is examined. For some starting values, escape occurs quickly, after a small number of repetitions.

For centuries, the Doom Bar was regarded as a significant danger to ships—to be approached with caution to avoid running aground. When sails were the main source of power, ships coming round Stepper Point would lose the wind, causing loss of steerage, leaving them to drift away from the channel. Sometimes, gusts of wind known colloquially as "flaws" blew over Stepper Point and pushed vessels towards the sandbank. Dropping anchor would not help, as it could not gain a firm hold on the sand. Richard Hellyer, the Sub-Commissioner of Pilotage at Padstow, gave evidence in 1859 that the Doom Bar was regarded as so dangerous that in a storm, vessels would risk being wrecked on the coast rather than negotiate the channel to Padstow harbour.

Richard Mentor Johnson was born on October 17, 1780, near present-day Louisville, Kentucky, in an area then known as Bryant's Station, Virginia. Clepsicosma is a genus of moths in the family Crambidae. As at 2022, this genus contains only one described species, Clepsicosma iridia, which is endemic to New Zealand. The species inhabits native forest in the North Island as well as the northern and western parts of the South Island down to Westland. The larval host of this species is assumed to be species of Cutty grass, possibly including Gahnia setifolia and Gahnia xanthocarpa, although the life history of this species is unknown. The adults of C. iridia are on the wing from December until May. They are nocturnal, and are attracted to light. During the day the adults rest on the underside of leaves, including those Cutty grass species that may possibly be their larval hosts.

A 2018 study found that all indigenous dogs originally brought by the first wave of human migration into the Americas, termed the "pre-contact dog" (PCD) clade, are now extinct. The group includes the dogs brought by Paleo-Eskimos 4,500 years ago. The Canadian Eskimo dogs, brought in a later wave by the Thule people, belong to the neighboring group of Arctic dogs. An 2019 study confirms that these later Arctic dogs were dispersed by the Inuit across North America 2000 years ago, replacing the first wave of PCDs where they go.

Although a standard English is generally used in public and official settings, a range of registers (stylistic levels) exists for various contexts, such as journalism (print, television, internet) and academic publishing (monographs, academic papers, internet). Registers also distinguish between spoken and written forms of Standard English, which vary in formality. Standard English features stylistic variations ranging from casual to formal. Additionally, the usage codes of nonstandard dialects (vernacular languages) are less stabilized and more readily accept and integrate new vocabulary and grammatical forms. National varieties of Standard English are characterized by generally accepted rules and grammars established by linguistic prescription in the 18th century.

Canajoharie Creek is a river in Central New York, United States. It is located in Otsego and Montgomery counties. The creek originates north of Cherry Valley and flows northward, eventually converging with the Mohawk River in Canajoharie.

The creek features two waterfalls, Judds Falls and Canajoharie Falls, and a deep pothole known as "the boiling pot." The name "Canajoharie" is derived from a Mohawk term referring to this pothole. The creek has a history of flash flooding, and the United States Geological Survey maintains stream gauges along its course. The creek is stocked with brown trout and is rated Class C for water quality.

On 24 May, more than 1,600 flights were grounded as ash clouds travelled over Scotland, with airports in Scotland and northern England closed and 250 UK flights grounded. British Airways, KLM, Aer Lingus, Flybe, BMI, Loganair, Eastern Airways, EasyJet, and Ryanair cancelled most flights in the Scotland region, seeing Wales' Cardiff Airport also cancelling inbound and outbound flights by airline Flybe. Certain flights from Northern Ireland were also grounded. The BBC has provided a list of affected flights to and from Irish airports. By nightfall, 20 flights had been cancelled to and from Göteborg Landvetter Airport, with anticipated wind shift bringing the ash clouds from southwestern Sweden to the northeast, possibly reaching Stockholm's Arlanda Airport by 02:00 on Wednesday morning. Furthermore, Continental Airlines Flights 75 and 97 (from Hamburg and Berlin Tegel to Newark Liberty) had to make stopovers at Gander Airport, Newfoundland, to refuel as they were assigned more southerly routes that exceeded the Boeing 757-200s' range.

U.S. Highway 59 in Texas is named the Lloyd Bentsen Highway, after Lloyd Bentsen, a former U.S. Senator and Secretary of the Treasury. In northern Houston, U.S. Highway 59 is co-signed with Interstate 69 and is known as the Eastex Freeway, running from downtown Houston to the Liberty County/Montgomery County line. To the south, it is referred to as the Southwest Freeway, which also shares the I-69 designation. This section supports a vehicle count of over 330,000 vehicles per day just outside the Loop, making it one of the busiest freeways in the United States.

Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil was a box office failure, grossing $25.1 million to an estimated $30 million budget. It received mixed reviews. It scores 51% on review aggregator Rotten Tomatoes, based on 37 reviews with an average rating of 6/10. The site's consensus states: "Clint Eastwood's spare directorial style proves an ill fit for this Southern potboiler, which dutifully trudges through its mystery while remaining disinterested in the cultural flourishes that gave its source material its sense of intrigue." On Metacritic, the film has a weighted average score of 57 out of 100, based on 23 critics, indicating "mixed or average reviews". Audiences polled by CinemaScore gave the film an average grade of "C+" on an A+ to F scale.

Sapporo Television Broadcasting Co., Ltd. (STV) is a private television and radio broadcasting company based in Sapporo, Japan. Founded on April 8, 1958, it began television broadcasts on April 1, 1959, initially as a dual affiliate of NNN and FNN. STV later became a founding member of NNN in 1966 and transitioned to color broadcasts the same year. It also played a key role in broadcasting the 1972 Sapporo Winter Olympics. 

STV launched its radio operations in 1962, becoming the only Japanese broadcaster to start with television before radio. The company introduced digital broadcasting in 2006 and ended analog broadcasts in 2011. Known for its innovative approaches, such as adopting an automatic advertising broadcast system, STV has consistently ranked first in TV ratings since 1993, holding the longest record for all-day ratings in Japan.

Wintley Phipps served as senior pastor to several churches in the Washington, D.C., metropolitan area, including the Capitol Hill and Seabrook Seventh-day Adventist Churches. They have three sons: Wintley II, Winston Adriel, and Wade Alexander.

Rappresentatione di anima et di corpo (Portrayal of the Soul and the Body) is a musical work by Emilio de' Cavalieri to a libretto by Agostino Manni (1548-1618). With it, Cavalieri regarded himself as the composer of the first opera or oratorio. Whether he was actually the first is subject to some academic debate, as is whether the work is better categorized as an opera or an oratorio. It was first performed in Rome in February 1600 in the Oratorio dei Filippini adjacent to the church of Santa Maria in Vallicella.

Occupy Wall Street (OWS') is a protest movement which began September 17, 2011 in Zuccotti Park, located in New York City's Wall Street financial district. The protest was initiated by the Canadian activist group Adbusters with the purpose of taking a stand against what is seen as social and economic inequality, high unemployment, greed, corruption, and the undue influence of large corporations—particularly from the financial services sector—on government. The protesters' slogan We are the 99% refers to the movement's perception of growing income and wealth inequality in the United States between the wealthiest 1% and the remaining 99% of the population.

The Abbey of San Pietro al Monte is an small medieval monastic complex mostly dating to the 11th century, in Lombard Romanesque style, on a hill outside the town of Civate, province of Lecco, in northern Italy.

Founded in 796 as a small hermitage by the Lombard king Desiderius, the complex was considerably expanded in the 11th century, and then left alone in terms of major building and decorating. It was secularized in the aftermath of the French Revolution. Its 11th-century features remain very largely intact, including many frescos and carved reliefs.

In 2016 it was added to the "tentative" list to be a UNESCO World Heritage Site, as part of a group of eight Italian medieval Benedictine monasteries, representing "The cultural landscape of the Benedictine settlements in medieval Italy".

Podéšť studied music composition at Brno Conservatory under Jaroslav Kvapil from 1941, graduating in 1948. In Brno, he also worked as director of the Radost Youth Choir, for whom he wrote a large number of choral works. For the years 1953–1956, Podéšť replaced Radim Drejsl (1923–1953) as director of the Vít Nejedlý Army Artistic Ensemble (Armádní umělecký soubor Víta Nejedlého) in Prague, then during 1958–1961, he worked as an editor of music broadcasts for Czechoslovak Television. After 1961 he devoted himself exclusively to composition, only occasionally working as a freelancer. From 1966 until his death, Podéšť lived in Morocco with his wife, who was a doctor.

The concept for the museum emerged in 2000 from a San Francisco Bay Area collaborative sculpture group called Meadowsweet Dairy. Members of the collective included Sam Bower, Genda Griffith, Henry Corning, Dan Ustin, Alan Leavitt, Tyler Johnson and Alex Tereshkin. These artists recognized the need for a cultural infrastructure to support ecological artwork and artists. Through a series of meetings, the name greenmuseum.org was arrived upon, and the collective applied for non-profit status. The site was launched on December 21, 2001.

Mastopexy (Greek μαστός mastos "breast" + -pēxiā "affix") is a plastic surgery procedure that modifies the size, contour, and position of breasts. The procedure typically involves reshaping breast tissue and repositioning the nipple-areola complex (NAC) to achieve a more elevated appearance. Surgeons consider tissue viability of the NAC to preserve sensation and potential for lactation. The operation may involve removing excess glandular tissue, fat, and skin, as well as tightening overstretched ligaments. The goal is to create a more proportionate bust line while maintaining breast function.

The Catskill Mountain House was a hotel built in 1824 on a plateau in the Catskill Mountains. It became a popular subject for artists and writers, including those of the Hudson River School. The hotel declined in popularity and was burned down by the State of New York in 1963. Today, the site offers sweeping views and is near the North-South Lake campground.

Jeff Unaegbu held the position of executive officer at the Lagos State Council of Tradesmen and Artisans, which is located within the Ministry of Commerce. His first book, "This Lagos Na Wa and Other Poetic Portraits," was published in January 2006 by Prize Publishers. The poem in this book, "This Lagos Na Wa," consists of approximately 4,895 lines and features a consistent rhyme scheme. Newswatch magazine reviewed the poem as "the longest poem by a Nigerian" on April 2, 2007. The poem was later declared "the longest metrical poem in Africa" by The Roar magazine. A revised edition of the book, titled "This Lagos Na Wa: Ode on Lagos and Other Poetic Portraits," was published by Pitmak Books in 2010, adding a prologue and epilogue to the original collection.

Owari province was one of the foremost production centres of enamel in the country. During the Edo period the Andō family operated a pipe shop called "Murata-ya". Andō Jubei (Jusaburo) (1876-1953) was born in Nagoya as the fourth child; he had three elder sisters. His mother died in May 1877 following an illness, and his father followed in September 1877. Orphaned at less than one year old, he was raised according to his father’s will by staff employers. His older sister married Andō Juzaemon, whose born name was Matsukichi. Together with his brother-in-law they made the cloisonné company a success. In 1893, Andō Juzaemon went to the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago. It was his first time to travel overseas, and he used the opportunity to study the market. In 1901 Andō Jubei went to the Glasgow International Exhibition, which was his first overseas travel, and he stayed for two years in a British home to study the market.

Spindizzy is an isometric computer game released for several 8-bit home computer formats in 1986 by Electric Dreams Software. It combines action and puzzle game elements. Players must navigate a series of screens to explore a landscape suspended in a three-dimensional space. Development was headed by Paul Shirley, who drew inspiration from Ultimate Play the Game games that feature an isometric projection.

The game was successful in the United Kingdom and was well received by the video game press. Reviewers praised its visuals and design, but criticized its audio. Similarities were drawn to Marble Madness, which was released in arcades two years earlier. Spindizzy was followed by a 1990s sequel titled Spindizzy Worlds.

William Broad is a prolific science journalist and author, having written or co-authored several books, including the number-one New York Times Best Seller *Germs: Biological Weapons and America's Secret War*, published in 2001. He co-wrote *Betrayers of the Truth: Fraud and Deceit in the Halls of Science* with Nicholas Wade in 1982. Broad's 1992 book is *The Science of Yoga: The Risks and the Rewards*, which examines yoga's benefits and potential injuries. Broad's writings have been translated into over fifty languages, reflecting his focus on scientific inquiry and public understanding of complex topics.

Lamakaan (Lang-ur) is an open cultural center in Banjara Hills, Hyderabad, India. The non-profit organisation provides space for events such as arts, literature, theater, music, and debates.

Sergio Llull (born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player for Real Madrid of the Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. Not yet of legal age to begin the practice of law, Morphy found himself with free time. He received an invitation to participate in the First American Chess Congress, to be held in New York from October 6 to November 10, 1857. He at first declined, but at the urging of his uncle eventually decided to play. He defeated each of his rivals, including James Thompson, Alexander Beaufort Meek, and two strong German masters, Theodor Lichtenhein and Louis Paulsen, the latter two in the semifinal and final rounds. Morphy was hailed as the chess champion of the United States, but he appeared unaffected by his sudden fame. According to the December 1857 issue of Chess Monthly, "his genial disposition, his unaffected modesty and gentlemanly courtesy have endeared him to all his acquaintances." In the fall of 1857, staying in New York, Morphy played 261 games, both regular and at odds. His overall score in regular games was 87 wins, 8 draws, and 5 losses.

**Promoting agribusiness, rural areas and history**  
Landwirtschaftliche Rentenbank, Germany's development agency for agribusiness and rural areas, provides special promotional loans focused on agriculture, renewable energy, and rural development. In addition to financing agribusiness, it supports regional authorities, banks, and EU-based institutions operating in rural areas. Its statutory promotional mandate includes funding for sustainable agriculture, AgTech and FoodTech startups, and projects like organic farming, emissions reduction, and animal welfare. Rentenbank extends loans through other banks on a competitively neutral basis, offering favorable terms. Beyond lending, it awards grants for agricultural innovations, applied research, and institutions vital to rural development. The Rehwinkel Foundation, affiliated with Rentenbank, supports scientific research, industry-academia collaboration, and scholarships for Master's students in agriculture-related fields. Civic engagement and public-sector investments target broadband expansion, rural tourism, and community wind farms, aligning with its mission to foster sustainable rural growth.

BaaS providers generate revenue from their services in various ways, often using a freemium model. Under this model, a client receives a certain number of free active users or API calls per month, and pays a fee for each user or call over this limit. Alternatively, clients can pay a set fee for a package which allows for a greater number of calls or active users per month. There are also flat fee plans that make the pricing more predictable. Some of the providers offer the unlimited API calls inside their free plan offerings. While BaaS is most commonly offered as a commercial service, open-source options are available.

Additionally, from the northwest, Vatutin’s 1st Ukrainian Front was to deploy the 27th and 40th Armies, with the 6th Tank Army to exploit penetrations supported by the 2nd Air Army.

"Burma VJ" is a Danish documentary film directed by Anders Østergaard, released on November 12, 2008. The film chronicles the 2007 Burmese protests through the eyes of local video journalists, highlighting issues of censorship and the struggle for freedom of speech. It received critical acclaim, boasting a 97% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes and winning several awards, including the Golden Apricot for Best Documentary Film at the 2009 Yerevan International Film Festival.

Lashkargāh (Lang-ps; Lang-fa), historically called Bost or Boost (بست، بوست), is a city in southwestern Afghanistan and the capital of Helmand Province. It is located in Lashkargah District, where the Arghandab River merges into the Helmand River. The city has a population of 201,546 as of 2006. Lashkargah is linked by major roads with Kandahar to the east, Zaranj on the border with Iran to the west, and Farah and Herat to the north-west. It is mostly very arid and desolate. However, farming does exist around the Helmand and Arghandab rivers. Bost Airport is located on the east bank of the Helmand River, five miles north of the junction of the Helmand and Arghandab rivers. Because of the trading hubs, it is Afghanistan's second largest city in size, after Kabul and before Kandahar.

After several weeks of fighting in the Battle of Lashkargah, the city was captured by the Taliban on 13 August 2021, becoming the fourteenth provincial capital to be seized by the Taliban as part of the wider 2021 Taliban offensive.

The Eighth Circuit affirmed that searches related to the seizure of materials were protected by Marcus's First Amendment rights. In 2022, the Fifth Circuit ruled that Marcus's protections did not apply to the seizure of material not related to expressive content, occurring 39 years after the original Marcus decision in 1983.

The Critérium du Dauphiné was created in 1947 by the French newspaper *Le Dauphiné Libéré* to boost its circulation after World War II. The newspaper was based in the Dauphiné region of southeastern France. The race was designed to precede the Tour de France on the cycling calendar, acting as a preparatory event. The inaugural edition of the race was won by Edouard Klabinski.

Local councils (Hebrew: plural: מוֹעָצוֹת מְקוֹמִיּוֹת‎ Mo'atzot Mekomiot / singular: מוֹעָצָה מְקוֹמִית‎ Mo'atza Mekomit, Arabic: plural: مجالس محليّة Majalis Mahaleea / singular: مجلس محلّي Majlis Mahalee) are one of the three types of local government found in Israel, the other two being cities and regional councils. Local councils should not be confused with local committees, which are lower-level administrative entities.

William W. Arnold (commonly "W. W."; August 28, 1843 – March 30, 1923) was a physician better known across the country for his avocation as an ornithologist. Born in Connersville, Indiana, he initially practiced medicine near Rushville, Indiana. In 1886, he visited Colorado and decided he would like to move there and he settled in Colorado Springs in October of that year. He operated a practice in downtown Colorado Springs and compounded medicine for his patients. He is best known as the founder and operator of a bird hospital, where he cared for ill and injured birds. He took care of domestic birds and had a large influx of wild birds after bad weather, like hail storms. He wrote articles about nature and birds, and he gave talks about them to school children in Colorado and Indiana. He worked as a physician and caregiver for the bird hospital until shortly before his death at age 79.

Clepsicosma iridia, described by Edward Meyrick in 1888, is a species of moth endemic to New Zealand. Found in both the North and South Islands, particularly in native forests, it is common and has a flight period from late December to May. The species' forewing features a wavy line of iridescent scales, named after the Roman goddess Iris. Clepsicosma iridia is believed to feed on species of Cutty grass, though this has not been confirmed through captive rearing. The moths are nocturnal and attracted to light, often resting under leaves during the day.

The Scandinavian languages underwent significant changes during the Middle Ages and Early Modern period, influenced notably by Low German. By the 18th century, written Danish had mostly found its modern form, based on the Copenhagen dialect. Copenhagen, the capital of Denmark–Norway in the 17th century, was central to this development. During the Dano-Norwegian union (1380–1814), Danish became the primary written language in Norway, especially among the elite. After the union dissolved in 1814, Danish was often referred to in Norway as "Dano-Norwegian," reflecting its role as the dominant written language during the union.

Daud Haider is a Bangladeshi poet known for his freethoughts and writings. He served as the literary editor of the well-known Dhaka daily, *Dainik Sambad*, the largest circulated Bengali daily in North-East India. Wilson's snipe (Gallinago delicata) is a small, stocky shorebird. The genus name gallinago is New Latin for a woodcock or snipe from Latin gallina, "hen" and the suffix -ago, "resembling". The specific delicata is Latin for "dainty".

This species was considered to be a subspecies of the common snipe (G. gallinago) until 2003 when it was given its own species status, though not all authorities recognized this immediately. Wilson's snipe differs from the latter species in having a narrower white trailing edge to the wings, and eight pairs of tail feathers instead of the typical seven of the common snipe. Its common name commemorates the American ornithologist Alexander Wilson.

Brodie Smith (born in Jacksonville, Florida) is an American professional Ultimate Disc League player, disc golfer, and trick shot performer. He began playing ultimate frisbee at Nease High School in Ponte Vedra, Florida, and later joined the University of Florida's ultimate team in 2006. During his college career, the team won two National College Championships, in 2006 and 2010. 

Smith started his professional ultimate career in 2011 with the Indianapolis Alleycats of the American Ultimate Disc League (AUDL) and later played for teams including the Chicago Wildfire, Dallas Roughnecks, and Jacksonville Cannons. His career was interrupted by injuries, including a torn ACL and meniscus, which sidelined him for two seasons.

Smith gained widespread recognition for his frisbee trick shot videos, starting with his 2011 YouTube video "Frisbee Trick Shots," which garnered millions of views. His creative and challenging trick shots have been featured on ESPN SportsCenter's Top Plays and have helped promote awareness of ultimate frisbee as a sport. Smith has performed trick shots worldwide, collaborated with YouTube group Dude Perfect, and appeared on a Japanese game show. He continues to share his content on social media platforms, inspiring fans globally.

MV *Empire Day* was a British CAM ship and later cargo vessel built in 1941 for the Ministry of War Transport. Operated by Stephens Sutton Ltd and later Lyle Shipping Co Ltd, she carried a Hawker Sea Hurricane and defensive armament for convoy protection. During World War II, she transported supplies across the Atlantic, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean. On 7 August 1944, she was torpedoed and sunk by U-198 off East Africa, though all but one of her crew survived.

The Qajar dynasty (also romanized as Ghajar, Kadjar, or Qachar) was an Iranian royal dynasty of Turkic origin from the Qajar tribe that ruled Iran from 1789 to 1925. The state was officially known as the Sublime State of Persia (later Iran). The Qajars gained control of Iran in 1794 after defeating Lotf 'Ali Khan, the last ruler of the Zand dynasty. In 1796, Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar captured Mashhad, ending the Afsharid dynasty, and was subsequently crowned shah. During the 19th century, the Qajar dynasty lost control of several territories in the Caucasus region, including parts of modern-day Georgia, Dagestan, Azerbaijan, and Armenia, to the Russian Empire through a series of conflicts and treaties.

After the Congress of Vienna divided the Italian peninsula among the European powers, the Carbonari spread into the Papal States, the Kingdom of Sardinia, the Grand Duchy of Tuscany, the Duchy of Modena, and the Kingdom of Lombardy-Venetia. The authorities passed an ordinance condemning to death anyone who attended a Carbonari meeting due to concerns about their influence. Despite this, the society continued to exist and was involved in various uprisings in Italy from 1820 onward. The Carbonari criticized Napoleon III for failing to unite Italy and attempted to assassinate him. Many leaders of the unification movement were associated with this organization.

For the spectator, the listener, and the viewer, the authenticity of experience is an emotion impossible to recapture beyond the first encounter with the work of art in its original setting. In the cases of sculpture and of painting, the contemporary visitor to a museum encounters the work of art displayed in a simulacrum of the original setting for which the artist created the art. To that end, the museum visitor will see a curated presentation of the work of art as an objet d’art, and might not perceive the aesthetic experience inherent to observing the work of art in its original setting — the intent of the artist.

Supersingular Isogeny Key Exchange (SIDH) is a post-quantum cryptographic key exchange protocol based on the mathematics of supersingular elliptic curves and isogenies. Introduced in 2011 by De Feo, Jao, and Plût, SIDH provides forward secrecy and resists attacks from both classical and quantum computers. Unlike traditional Diffie-Hellman, SIDH relies on computing isogenies (rational maps between elliptic curves) rather than discrete logarithms, making it secure against Shor's algorithm.  

The protocol involves two parties exchanging public keys derived from secret isogenies, allowing them to compute a shared secret (the j-invariant of a resulting elliptic curve). SIDH was initially believed to offer strong security, with classical and quantum attack complexities of \(O(p^{1/4})\) and \(O(p^{1/6})\), respectively. However, in 2022, Castryck and Decru developed an efficient key-recovery attack exploiting auxiliary points in SIDH public keys, breaking proposed instantiations like SIKE.  

Despite this vulnerability, SIDH remains notable for its compact key sizes (~564 bytes) and efficient implementations, including constant-time software by Microsoft. It has influenced research in isogeny-based cryptography, including digital signatures and designated-verifier schemes.

In 1872, the legislature filed impeachment charges against the incumbent Republican governor, Henry Clay Warmoth, over disputes over certifying returns of the disputed gubernatorial election, in which both Democrat John McEnery and Republican William Kellogg claimed victory. Trying to support a centrist fusion government at a time of divisions among Republicans, Warmoth had supported his appointed return board, which certified McEnery as winner. Republicans opposed this outcome and appointed their own returns board, which certified Kellogg. The election had been marked by violence and fraud. Under Article 53 of the Louisiana Constitution of 1868, Pinchback—then lieutenant governor—assumed the governorship on December 9, 1872, after Warmoth was impeached and removed from office. He served for 36 days, completing Warmoth’s term until January 13, 1873, becoming the first African American U.S. state governor. The constitution permitted the lieutenant governor to succeed in cases of impeachment, removal, death, resignation, or absence. Though Warmoth was not convicted and charges were dropped, Pinchback’s brief tenure marked a historic milestone, remaining the only Black governor during Reconstruction until 1989.

**Blackout policy**  
Major League Baseball (MLB) enforces blackout rules to prioritize local broadcasters over national ones when televising in-market team games. Local broadcasters retain exclusive rights unless a game is non-exclusive, allowing both local and national broadcasts. In such cases, national feeds may be blacked out locally. These restrictions also apply to MLB.tv, preventing in-market streaming even if local cable providers lack the regional network.

Pokémon Sun and Moon are role-playing video games set in the tropical, Hawaii-inspired Alola region. Presented from the perspective of a visitor exploring this vibrant locale, the games follow a young trainer who leaves their home to embark on a journey to become a Pokémon Master. Players capture wild Pokémon using Poké Balls, a staple mechanic of the series, and their Pokémon gain experience points both through battles and by capturing other Pokémon, a feature reintroduced in Pokémon X and Y. The games emphasize storytelling more heavily than previous entries, with a plot-driven narrative that complements the traditional gameplay elements of battling, training, and exploring. Players can also engage in battles and trade Pokémon with others locally or online, fostering a sense of community and strategy. Additionally, the games replace traditional Hidden Machines (HMs) with a new system that allows players to summon specific Pokémon for traversal and utility purposes, streamlining exploration. This combination of features makes Pokémon Sun and Moon a fresh yet familiar experience for fans of the series.

The Platte River Campground Site, designated 20BZ16, is an archaeological site located along the Platte River, within the Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore near Empire, Michigan. Many linguistic sources relating to the Slavic languages describe Silesian as a dialect of Polish. However, many Silesians consider themselves a separate ethnicity and have been advocating the recognition of Silesian as a distinct language. According to the last official census in Poland in 2011, over 500 thousand people declared Silesian as their native language. Many sociolinguistic sources (e.g. Tomasz Kamusella assert that the determination between a language or a dialect is ultimately a matter of extralinguistic criteria, such as national attachment or the political attitudes of its users, and this changes over time. See: Agnieszka Pianka, Alfred F. Majewicz, Tomasz Wicherkiewicz) Language organizations such as SIL International and various linguistic resources such as Ethnologue, and Poland's Ministry of Administration and Digitization, recognize Silesian as a distinct language. In 2007, Silesian was assigned its language code szl within the ISO 639-3 standard.

Since 1875, American medical doctor John Harvey Kellogg in Battle Creek, Michigan, at his huge sanitarium—advertised as "University of Health", staffing some 800 to 1 000, and yearly receiving several thousand patients, including US Presidents and celebrities—had battled degeneration and disease by fending off bowel sepsis. In the early 20th century, rebuking alleged "health faddists" like Kellogg and Sylvester Graham, American physicians who embraced focal infection theory cast themselves in the German tradition of "scientific medicine". Kellogg argued that German researchers ostensibly repudiated the autointoxication principle, but, by using different terminology, supported it circuitously. Since French pathologist Charles Jacques Bouchard, in his 1887 book, coined the term autointoxication, French researchers had investigated and openly advocated the principle, already presaged by multiple researchers in Europe and America. Meanwhile, British surgeons still knife-happy, Hunter warned of "intestinal stasis" impairing mental stability, and called for "surgical bacteriology".

The second accident at the National Research Universal (NRU) reactor occurred in 1958, involving a serious incident characterized by a fuel rod rupture and subsequent fire within the reactor building. During this accident, several metallic uranium fuel rods overheated and ruptured, leading to one rod catching fire as it was being removed using a robotic crane. As the crane arm moved away from the reactor vessel, a three-foot length of burning uranium broke off and fell into a shallow maintenance pit. The result of this breakage was a significant fire, which was extinguished by crew members in protective gear who sprayed the area with water, although initial attempts were largely unsuccessful.

Islamist Shi'ism is a modern interpretation of Twelver Shia Islam that incorporates elements of Muslim Brotherhood ideologies and the mysticism of Ibn Arabi. This perspective emphasizes Islam as a political system and differs from mainstream Usuli and Akhbari traditions in its support for establishing an Islamist state during the occultation of the twelfth Imam. Hadi Khosroshahi was among the first to identify as an Islamist Shia. While traditional Shia theology maintains a more secular approach during the occultation period, Islamist Shi'ites have adapted certain Sunni Islamist concepts to fit within a Shia framework. The origins of this movement trace back to early 20th century Iran during the Constitutional Revolution, when Shaikh Fazlullah Nouri's political stance contrasted with that of Akhund Khurasani, a prominent Usuli scholar of the time.

The Theory of Forms typically refers to the belief expressed by Socrates in some of Plato's dialogues, that the material world as it seems to us is not the real world, but only an image or copy of the real world. Socrates spoke of forms in formulating a solution to the problem of universals. The forms, according to Socrates, are roughly speaking archetypes or abstract representations of the many types of things, and properties we feel and see around us, that can only be perceived by reason ; (that is, they are universals). In other words, Socrates sometimes seems to recognise two worlds, the natural world which is constantly changing, and is merely an imperfect reflection, of the permanent and heavenly world of ideas (forms).

The William S. DeLano House, designed in the Georgian architectural style, features a stone foundation and utilizes intricate brickwork construction. The main block of the house is two stories tall, with a single-story wing fronted by a columned porch. The porch is supported by full classical columns, reflecting the Greek Revival style. Additional Greek Revival-style trim elements include bas-reliefs and limestone quoins framing the brick facades, contributing to the house's refined aesthetic. This design exemplifies Delano's adaptation of traditional architectural models.

The Vietnam War introduced serious tensions into the general's family life. His son Dennis fled to India to escape service. Nevertheless, General Carroll donned his uniform to represent Dennis, after he returned from India, before the Selective Service board and succeeded in getting his son recognized as a conscientious objector. There were also serious disagreements with his son James, who was ordained to the Roman Catholic priesthood in 1969. Father and son first clashed over the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.; the general suspected several of King's aides were Communists, whereas James admired King as a champion of the poor. James' anti-war activity was an embarrassment to his father. After leaving the priesthood, James became a journalist and author. Several of his novels, including Prince of Peace (1984) and Memorial Bridge (1991), bear traces of the general's fraught relations with his sons, albeit in fictionalized form. James Carroll's memoir, An American Requiem: God, My Father, and the War That Came Between Us, winner of the National Book Award in 1996, addressed his differences with his father directly.

In November 2008, a partially preserved skull, as well as teeth and the lower jaw, belonging to L. melvillei, the holotype specimen MUSM 1676, were discovered in the coastal desert of Peru in the sediments of the Pisco Formation, 35 km (22 mi) southwest of the city of Ica. Klaas Post, a researcher for the Natural History Museum Rotterdam in the Netherlands, stumbled across them on the final day of a field trip. Following World War I, areas designated by the Allied states as Slovak became part of the new state of Czechoslovakia, as per the Treaty of Trianon, to which Hungary was forced to agree. This arrangement left a significant Hungarian minority (~1,000,000 at that time) in Slovakia, similar to the German minority in the Czech lands, and a smaller Slovak minority in Hungary. The Hungarian Soviet Republic subsequently attempted to invade Slovakia but was defeated by a Czechoslovak-Romanian coalition.
HMS Ardent was a Royal Navy 27 knot torpedo boat destroyer ordered from John I Thornycroft & Company under the 1893 – 1894 Naval Estimates. She was the sixth ship to carry this name.

The Jim Henson Hour is a television series that aired on NBC in 1989. It was developed as a showcase for The Jim Henson Company's puppet creations, including Muppet characters. Nine of the twelve produced episodes aired before the program was cancelled.

Mafdet was an ancient Egyptian goddess associated with protection, justice, and execution. She was believed to defend the sun god Ra during his nightly voyage, earning the epithet "Piercer of Darkness," and played a role in reassembling Osiris after his dismemberment. Mafdet was often depicted as a feline or mongoose, a woman with the head of such an animal, or an animal with a woman's head, with the feline commonly interpreted as a cheetah or serval. In art, she was shown running up the side of an executioner's staff, symbolizing her role in delivering justice by ripping out the hearts of wrongdoers and presenting them to the pharaoh. During the New Kingdom, she was associated with the judgment hall in Duat, where she executed the enemies of the pharaoh.

Currently, some electricity is imported to Finland. In recent years, a varying amount (5–17 percent) of power has been imported from Russia, Sweden and Norway. Russia's plans for a submarine power cable have raised national security concerns, leading the government to deny permits. Neste operates two large oil refineries in Finland, serving transport, business, and consumer markets. Refined products account for 22% of Finland's chemical exports.

The Me Too Movement (or #MeToo Movement) is a movement against sexual assault and harassment, especially in the workplace. The movement followed public revelations of sexual misconduct allegations against Harvey Weinstein. Tarana Burke, an American social activist and community organizer, began using the phrase "Me Too" as early as 2006, and it was reportedly popularized by American actress Alyssa Milano on Twitter in 2017.
