Earl Hunt, writing about the research of hereditarians in this area, states that because brain size is found to have a correlation of about.35 with intelligence among whites and is almost entirely genetically determined, race differences in average brain size are therefore an important argument for a possible genetic contribution to racial IQ gaps. However as brain images are expensive to obtain, much of the research in this area is based on measures of cranial capacity, which only measures brain size indirectly and thus makes the data less reliable. Hunt notes that, even when combined with measures of processing speed (mental chronometry), this data still just accounts for a difference of.19 standard deviations between Black and White average test scores, only a portion of the 1.0 standard deviation gap in average scores that is observed.

Born in Workington, Cumbria, Dobie joined Carlisle United as an apprentice in June 1995, turning professional on 10 May 1997. While at Carlisle, it was Dobie's goalbound header that was parried into the path of Carlisle goalkeeper Jimmy Glass, who scored with the last kick of the final game of the 1998–99 season, preserving Carlisle's Football League status and relegating Scarborough. During 2000–01, Dobie attracted interest from other clubs, with Carlisle manager Ian Atkins claiming that the player could be worth as much as £2 million in the transfer market.

Saibal Chatterjee of NDTV gave it one and a half stars out of five and called the film "an egregiously escapist campus-rumpus musical in which nattily attired, unbelievably idle boys and skimpily clad girls... play games that make as much sense as a camel race on an airport tarmac", adding, "There isn't a single moment in the film that could be described as passable". Shubhra Gupta of *The Indian Express* also gave the film one and a half stars out of five, criticizing its predictability and lack of freshness. She described the "class of 2019" as stilted and expressed disappointment, expecting more "swish and sparkle" from Karan Johar. While she acknowledged Tiger Shroff's efficient execution of dances and fights, she noted his struggle to appear student-like, further contributing to the film's lackluster impact.

Wizzard Brew is the debut album by rock group Wizzard, released in 1973 on EMI's Harvest label. It reached a peak of No. 29 in the UK Albums Chart. In the United States, it was released by United Artists Records as Wizzard's Brew (with a different cover photo) but failed to chart there.

In 2003, Mojo magazine ranked it number 18 on its list of the "Top 50 Eccentric Albums".

However, after Stalin's death, the terror ended abruptly. Nikita Khrushchev initiated legal reforms and reorganized the police apparatus, the political police were brought under the control of the Communist Party, and the gulag was dismantled. Still, even in the era of glasnost ushered in by Mikhail Gorbachev nearly 30 years later, the organs of internal security still had a key role to play in protecting the Soviet regime from internal and external threats.

The Black Country derby is a football rivalry between West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers, two clubs located in the Black Country region of England. One of the oldest derbies in league football, it began in 1883 and has seen a total of 162 meetings, with Albion winning 65 times and Wolves 53. The rivalry peaked in the 1950s when both teams competed at the top level, and it has continued to evoke strong local passion, despite fluctuations in league status.

This newly revamped trio of Sparkes, Skarendahl, and Sharland subsequently signed to RCA and released their first album The Trick to Life on 22 October 2007. The band have spoken of their desire to write songs that are about more than just "boy and girl... finding love on a Friday night on the lash with your mates". In February 2008, they were named as Worst Band at the annual NME awards. They were produced by ex-member of Jamiroquai, Toby Smith, until his death in 2017, and managed by Steve Morton.

The Qajar dynasty (also romanised as Ghajar, Kadjar, Qachar, etc.) was a Persian royal dynasty of Turkic origin, specifically from the Qajar tribe, which ruled Persia (Iran) from 1785 to 1925. The state ruled by the dynasty was officially known as the Sublime State of Persia. The Qajar family took control of Persia in 1794, deposing Lotf 'Ali Khan, the last of the Zand dynasty, and reasserted Iranian sovereignty over parts of the Caucasus. In 1796, Mohammad Khan Qajar seized Mashhad, ending the Afsharid dynasty, and was formally crowned as shah after a campaign against Persia's Georgian subjects. The Qajar dynasty lost many territories in the Caucasus to Russia during the 19th century, including areas that are now part of Georgia, Dagestan, Azerbaijan, and Armenia.

Randolph's political career was chequered and he could perhaps be described as someone with talent who completely wasted it, mostly by unacceptably immature behaviour of which the worst trait was rudeness. He made an enemy of Robert Cecil, 3rd Marquess of Salisbury (1830–1903), who first became Prime Minister in 1885. Salisbury probably summarised Randolph very aptly when he described him as the "antithesis of the Mahdi" – Muhammad Ahmad (1848–1885). Salisbury said the Mahdi "pretends to be half mad and is very sane in reality". Salisbury nevertheless appointed Randolph as Chancellor of the Exchequer from 3 August 1886. Randolph proceeded to present budget proposals that went against the grain of Tory policy and faced opposition from the rest of the Cabinet. Outraged, Randolph sent Salisbury a letter of resignation on 22 December. Nevertheless, he arrogantly expected the Cabinet to back down so that he could continue on his own terms. Salisbury accepted his resignation and Randolph was out of frontline politics, never (unlike Winston) to return. He died young, aged 45, of a debilitating illness that may have been syphilis.

Frederick Twort (22 October 1877 – 20 March 1950) was an English bacteriologist known for his discovery of bacteriophages, viruses that infect and destroy bacteria. Educated at St Thomas's Hospital, he became superintendent of the Brown Animal Sanatory Institution, where he conducted groundbreaking research on bacterial mutation, growth factors, and diseases like leprosy and Johne's disease. His 1915 discovery of bacteriophages was initially overlooked but later recognized as a major contribution to microbiology. Twort was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1929.

The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a light aircraft designed and manufactured by Aeroprakt in Kyiv, Ukraine. Development began in February 1990, with the first prototype flying on October 21, 1996, and production of a German-certified version starting in 1999. The A-22 features a predominantly metal structure, excellent visibility through extensive glazing, and a 3-axis control system. It is equipped with flaperons, allowing for a low stall speed of 52 km/h (32 mph). The aircraft can be powered by various engines, including the Rotax 912UL and Jabiru 2200.

When NOAA was established in 1970 and the Coast and Geodetic Survey's assets became a part of NOAA, NOAA based its own flag on that of the Coast and Geodetic Survey. The NOAA flag is, in essence, the Coast and Geodetic Survey flag, with the NOAA logo—a circle divided by the silhouette of a seabird into an upper dark blue and a lower light blue section, but with the "NOAA" legend omitted—centered within the red triangle. NOAA ships in commission display the NOAA flag; those with only one mast fly it immediately beneath the ship's commissioning pennant or the personal flag of a civilian official or flag officer if one is aboard the ship, while multi masted vessels fly it at the masthead of the forwardmost mast. NOAA ships fly the same ensign as United States Navy ships but fly the NOAA flag as a distinguishing mark to differentiate themselves from Navy ships.

The Dauphiné is raced over 8 days in the Rhône-Alpes region in the southeast of France, traditionally covering portions of the French Alps. The race has often, but not always, started with an opening prologue on Sunday. The Monday and Tuesday stages are usually held in the lower hilly regions of Rhône-Alpes, before addressing the high mountains in the second half of the Dauphiné. Often there is one long individual or team time trial included.

Shaheed Lateef (11 June 1913 – 16 April 1967) was Hindi film director, writer, and producer. He was the maker of films like Ziddi (1948) which launched Dev Anand's career and Arzoo (1950) starring Dilip Kumar and Kamini Kaushal.

Social liberal policies have been adopted in many countries by governments that aim to address economic and social issues such as poverty, health care, education, and climate change through government intervention, while also emphasizing the rights and autonomy of the individual. Social liberal ideas and parties are often associated with leftist ideologies.

The plaintiff's principal allegations were that the UK government had been negligent in failing to become sufficient as a country in the supply of blood products at an earlier point, and in delaying the implementation of heat-treatment of clotting factors in order to inactivate HIV. It was further alleged that the defendants did not respond correctly to the AIDS crisis, specifically, in not reacting with urgency to move to banning imported blood products which were derived from paid donors. Another significant ground was breach of duty as provided for in statute, for example, under the National Health Service Act 1977.

Rouse has 18 credits as a screenwriter between 1942 and 1988. Starting with The Town Went Wild (1944), Rouse co-wrote many stories and scripts with Clarence Greene. The partners are noted for their work on a series of six film noirs, starting with D.O.A. (directed by Rudolph Maté-1949). With the second film in the series, The Well (1951), they also took on directing and producing: Rouse as director, and Greene as producer. This collaboration continued through the noir series (The Thief (1952), Wicked Woman (1953), New York Confidential (1955), and House of Numbers (1957)). In the late 1950s, Greene and Rouse formed Greene-Rouse Productions, which created the television series Tightrope that ran for one season (1959–1960) as well as two films in the 1960s.

The 1999 Brickyard 400 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series race held at Indianapolis Motor Speedway. The race was won by Dale Jarrett, who led 117 of the 160 laps, becoming the second two-time winner of the event. Jeff Gordon won the pole position. The race was broadcast on ABC with a Nielsen rating of 4.6/15.
Andrea Batista Schlesinger (born October 27, 1976) is an American political writer and campaigner. She was until 2009 the executive director of the Drum Major Institute.

Dardanella was a touring theatre company from the Dutch East Indies (modern-day Indonesia) established by Willy A. Piedro in 1926. Arising from a background of musical theatre, the troupe focused on realistic stories, both adaptations of foreign works and original stage plays about life in the Indies. Starring Dewi Dja' and Tan Tjeng Bok, the troupe performed original works by Piedro and Andjar Asmara. Popular both in the Indies and abroad, Dardanella dissolved during an international tour after 1936. Several of its members later went into film.

"Flash" is a song by the Japanese girl group Perfume, released as a single on March 16, 2016, and later included in their album *Cosmic Explorer*. Produced by Yasutaka Nakata, the song blends J-pop and EDM elements and features choreography inspired by martial arts and Tai chi. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, incorporates CGI and LED light sabers, becoming Perfume's most-watched video on YouTube with over 60 million views.

The non-Arab Syriac Christian groups, composed largely of Chaldo-Assyrians, form a significant portion of Christians in Iraq, northeast Syria, southeast Turkey, and northwest Iran. They are often defined as non-Arab ethnic groups, including by the governments of Iraq, Iran, and Turkey, and have their own native dialects of the Syriac-Aramaic language, in addition to sometimes speaking local Arabic or Turkish dialects. Some sources have referred to them as "Christians of the Arab World" or "Arabic Christians," which has been a topic of debate regarding their identity.

In 2014, Kevin Yoder ran for re-election to represent Kansas' 3rd Congressional District. He faced no opposition in the Republican primary and defeated Democrat Kelly Kultala, a former state senator and Wyandotte County commissioner, in the general election, winning 60% of the vote. According to the Center for Responsive Politics, Yoder received $53,257 from the payday-loan industry during the 2014 election cycle, making it a significant contributor to his campaign committee and leadership PAC.

The 12-inch version, remixed by Shep Pettibone, also appears on the compilation Substance and a second remix by Stephen Hague features on their Best Of album. The original album version appears on the 2005 compilation Singles, the 7-inch version appears on the 2016 reissue of this compilation. New Order's live versions since 1998 are based on the Shep Pettibone remix.

Ahead of the IPL 2014 auction, on 10 January 2014, the Delhi Daredevils announced that they would not retain any players from their current squad for season seven. With no players retained, the team had the most "right-to-match" cards among all the franchises at the auction—three. They also had Rs 600 million (approx US$9.6) to spend at the auction.

The date of construction of Lisbon's first pillory is unknown, but was probably in or before 1179 following the granting of a charter to Lisbon by Afonso I of Portugal. The earliest written records date for that structure date to late 14th century. Later records place the pillory at various places in the city; but all traces of this and many other structures were destroyed in the Lisbon earthquake of 1755.

Alexandru Macedonski was stripped of his administrative office by the Brătianu cabinet, a decision that plunged him into financial difficulties. Forced to relocate, he moved to a house on the outskirts of Bucharest and later shifted between residences in the northern part of the city. Despite his precarious finances, Macedonski continued to indulge in luxury and invested passionately in the decorative arts. The exact source of his income during this period remains unclear, though some speculated about assistance from European ruling houses. Poet Victor Eftimiu alleged that Macedonski employed questionable financial strategies, including sending flattering or threatening notes to influential figures and orchestrating marriages or relationships between his disciples and wealthy, aging women to extract their assets. These claims, alongside his persistent financial struggles, highlight the lengths Macedonski reportedly went to in order to sustain his lifestyle and artistic pursuits.

On her maiden cruise, from 23 November 1923 – 9 April 1924, Concord called at Mediterranean ports, passed through the Suez Canal to round the Cape of Good Hope, and exercised with the fleet in the Caribbean before returning to Philadelphia. As flagship of Commander, Destroyer Squadrons, Scouting Fleet, she cruised the Caribbean and sailed through the Panama Canal to exercise in the Hawaiian Islands in 1924–1925. Continuing to operate in the Atlantic, she joined in the Presidential Fleet Review taken by Calvin Coolidge on 4 June 1927.

The directives issued to the Luftwaffe for the Polish Campaign were to prevent the Polish Air Force to influence the ground battles, or to perform attacks on German territory. In addition, it was to support the advance of the German ground forces through direct tactical and indirect air support with attacks against Polish mobilization centres and thus delay an orderly Polish strategic concentration of forces and to deny mobility for Polish reinforcements through destruction of strategic Polish rail routes. Preparations were made for a concentrated attack (Operation Wasserkante) by all bomber forces against targets in Warsaw. The bombing of rail network, crossroads and troop concentrations played havoc on Polish mobilisation, while attacks upon civilian and military targets in towns and cities disrupted command and control by wrecking the antiquated Polish signal network: Shortly after, in a period of a few days, Luftwaffe numerical and technological superiority took its toll on the Polish Air Force.

Daniel J. Berthiaume (born January 26, 1966) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey goaltender who played six seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) with the Winnipeg Jets, Minnesota North Stars, Los Angeles Kings, Boston Bruins, and Ottawa Senators.

Summoning is an atmospheric black metal band formed in 1993 in Vienna, Austria, by members Silenius (Michael Gregor), Protector (Richard Lederer), and Trifixion (Alexander Trondl). Known for their epic and hypnotic sound, the band initially released traditional black metal music with their debut album, *Lugburz*, in 1995. Over the years, Summoning evolved their style, incorporating keyboards and choir vocals, heavily influenced by the works of J.R.R. Tolkien. The band has been active since its formation, with notable albums including *Minas Morgul*, *Oath Bound*, and *With Doom We Come*, released in 2018.

Glenn Gamboa of Newsday wrote that the stage set of massive cubes was similar to the Watch the Throne Tour by Jay-Z and Kanye West and noted that the "complicated" system of screens which was used to leave an impression that Beyoncé and her dancers were stepping in and out of black-and-white images was influenced by the opening credits of James Bond films. Tris McCall compared the light-up platforms to the towering cubes from the Watch the Throne Tour, noting that they served as screens for mesmerizing digital projections. He praised the light show as "hypnotic," enhancing the overall experience. McCall described the costumes as elegant yet striking, contributing to the visual spectacle. He also remarked on the powerful bass at Ovation Hall, stating, "I'm not sure I was ready for that jelly." Chuck Darrow, who has spent nearly 40 years covering the Philadelphia/Atlantic City entertainment scene, wrote for the Philadelphia Daily News, highlighting the impressive integration of technology and visuals in the performance.

French fries (American English, sometimes capitalized as "French" fries), also called chips, fries, or French-fried potatoes, are thin strips of deep-fried potato. In North America, the term "fries" typically refers to any elongated pieces of fried potatoes, while in the United Kingdom, Australia, Ireland and New Zealand, long thin-cut fried potatoes are often called fries to distinguish them from thicker-cut strips called chips. In French, they are known as frites, patates frites or pommes frites, a name also used in some non-French-speaking regions, while other languages use terms meaning "fried potatoes" or "French potatoes."

In July 1994, Howard Smith Limited bought BBC Hardware from Burns Philp and the chain was rebranded as Hardwarehouse. Subsequently, the chain expanded by opening new stores in Melbourne, Perth and Queensland. This followed with the chain's expansion into New Zealand. Since 1994, Hardwarehouse opened up to 14 stores a year. By 2001, the chain had 59 stores in Australia and 3 stores in New Zealand.

After a trial, Ajdarević joined Hereford United on loan until the end of the season, linking up with Leicester teammate Craig King on 25 March 2010. Ajdarević made his debut for the club on 3 April 2010, where he came on as a substitute and played 16 minutes, in a 2–1 win over Barnet. This turns out to be his only appearance for Hereford United, as he suffered a hamstring injury.

Studies in Latin American cities have shown that consuming water that has not been treated with adequate sanitation services introduces many human health issues to the public, especially to residents of poor and informal settlements. Low-income urban residents consuming polluted water are more likely to become ill with "poverty diseases" such as gastrointestinal and infectious water-borne diseases, as well as chronic diseases that degrade human health over time. Consumption of contaminated water and lack of access to clean water are also associated with health disorders that stem from emotional and physical stress and water-use conflicts. One of the most notable disease outbreaks in Latin America was the region's 1991-1993 cholera epidemic.

Parmalat S.p.A. is a dairy and food corporation which is a subsidiary of French multinational company Lactalis. It was founded by Calisto Tanzi in 1961.

Having become the leading global company in the production of long-life milk using ultra-high-temperature processing, the company collapsed in 2003 with a €14bn ($20bn; £13bn) hole in its accounts in what remains Europe's biggest bankruptcy. Since 2011, it has been a subsidiary of French group Lactalis (with fuller control since 2019). Today, Parmalat is a company with a global presence, having operations in Europe, North America, South America, Australia, China, and South Africa.

Still specializing in UHT milk and milk derivatives (varieties of yogurt, cheese, butter, ice cream, etc.), the group also sells fruit juices distributed under the brand names Lactis, Santal, Malù, and Kyr. Its worldwide operations include almost 140 production centres and some 16,000 employees, with 5,000 Italian dairy farms supplying it. Its shares were listed on the Borsa Italiana.

Sky and BBC News reported that the East London Mosque on Whitechapel Road in Whitechapel had been surrounded by armed police and that residents were told to stay indoors. The mosque was evacuated at about 10:30 and searched. However, police confirmed that it was a bomb scare and the all-clear was given after just over an hour.

Connah's Quay Power Station in Flintshire, Wales, is a 1,420 MW natural gas-fired power station. Originally a coal-fired station opened in 1954, it was replaced by a CCGT station in 1996, now owned by Uniper.

Painting the nails with colored nail polish (also called nail lacquer and nail varnish) to improve the appearance is a common practice dating back to at least 3000 B.C. Analysts note that smartphone dependence has fueled trends like the "nelfie," where clients paint self-portraits on gel nails to share online. Gel nails, cured under UV/LED light for durability, create extensions, art, or glossy finishes without sticky residue. They last 2–4 weeks, outperforming regular polish (1–2 weeks). Hardened gels offer flexibility and natural looks, making them popular for long-lasting manicures.

The suspect, later identified as Wen Ho Lee, pleaded guilty to a single charge of improper handling of national defense information, the 58 other counts against him were dropped, and he was released from jail on September 13, 2000. "Judge Parker apologized for Lee's 9-month solitary confinement, admitting he was misled by unproven espionage charges."

Butowsky founded the private wealth management firm Chapwood Investments in 2005. He is currently a managing partner in Chapwood Investments and created the Chapwood Index. By 2013, he was managing investments for a hundred professional athletes. Butowsky started the celebrity and athlete investor group Clubhouse Investment Club with professional baseball player Torii Hunter. Butowsky was also a managing director at Bear Stearns.

Cape Town, South Africa, is considered by many as a strong candidate for the 2020 Olympics due to its global prominence, excellent infrastructure, and beautiful setting. In preparation for the 2010 FIFA World Cup, the city is enhancing its world-class sporting facilities and expanding its hospitality infrastructure. Many believe the success of the 2010 FIFA event could boost Cape Town's bid for the 2020 Olympic Games.

In 1942, Nilsson was engaged to manage the Märte Måås-Fjetterström weaving studio in Båstad. She remained there for the next 30 years. Winning acclaim as "the number one Swedish textile artist", Nilsson brought in a number of her former students from the Stockholm Technical School, including Marianne Richter, Ann-Mari Forsberg [sv] and Barbro Sprinchorn [sv]. In 1943, she created one of her most successful rug designs Snäckorna, the first of a series in which she employed the haute-lisse technique.

A 24-ft (7 m) replica of a rearing Trigger was produced to sit atop the Roy Rogers Museum in Victorville. The 1,300-lb (600 kg) replica could be seen from the freeway and served as a landmark until the museum closed and moved to Branson. When the fiberglass replica of Trigger was being made, Rogers was approached by the owners of the Denver Broncos. He allowed another statue to be made for them, then broke the mold. "Bucky the Bronco", Trigger's twin, stands above the south scoreboard of Empower Field at Mile High (formerly Broncos Stadium).

Game II of 1991 is remembered for a number of dramatic incidents. Played in drenching rain, the game exploded into violence before half-time when volatile New South Wales forward Mark Geyer chopped down on Queensland hooker Steve Walters in a tackle. Prop Andrew Gee ran in to assist his teammate, sparking a brawl which involved most players from both teams. When the dust settled, referee David Manson issued a handful of cautions, but as players left the field for half-time, Queensland captain Wally Lewis continued to goad Geyer, clearly expecting the New South Wales forward to be antagonised into a violent response right in front of Manson that would have him sent off. This became a lasting image in Australian sporting folklore.

William Scott Husel, known as Billy Husel, was born on November 11, 1975, in Cleveland, Ohio. A physician, Husel served as an intensive-care doctor at Mount Carmel West Hospital in Columbus, Ohio. From February 2015 to November 2018, he allegedly prescribed excessive fentanyl to patients, resulting in 34 deaths. Accused of 25 counts of murder, Husel was acquitted in April 2022. Following the trial, Husel surrendered his medical license in Ohio, which was officially revoked on May 5, 2022.

Subsequently, he has had several notable roles in motion pictures, including Romeo + Juliet and Bring It On, playing the romantic interest. In 2002, he appeared as the lead in two films — Clockstoppers and Swimfan. He also had a minor role as White House intern Ryan Pierce for nine episodes during the fifth season of The West Wing.

Relations have been complex. The United States has historically assisted New Zealand in times of turmoil; for instance, during World War II and with the 2010 Canterbury earthquake. New Zealand has reciprocated; for example, by participating in the Vietnam War.

In 1909, he reportedly stayed up one night and taught himself to play the guitar at the age of 21. He became skilled in pre-Piedmont fingerpicking and slide guitar techniques. He continued working on the farm until 1916, when he was shot during an argument with his brother-in-law, as a result of which he lost his right leg, and had to wear a prosthetic afterwards.

It is known for its all-ages dances, festivals, quiet roads, and scenery. Yoga, gardening, and volunteerism are common activities. Many artists live on Denman Island, contributing to a local artistic community.

On September 16, 2022, Othram identified the remains as those of 14-year-old Joan "Joanie" Marie Dymond, who vanished from her home in Wilkes-Barre on June 25, 1969. Contemporary inquiries searched for the teenager in Atlantic City, New Jersey and New York City, but nothing turned up and her case went cold. Due to the circumstances her remains were found in, police suspect that she could have been a homicide victim and are investigating further.

In March 1930, Dominican President Horacio Vásquez was overthrown in a coup led by Rafael Estrella Ureña and General Rafael Trujillo. Estrella Ureña succeeded Vásquez as provisional president, and with Trujillo worked out an arrangement whereby the pair would stand on the same ticket at the 1930 presidential election (with Trujillo running for president and Estrella Ureña for vice-president). Brache, a supporter of the coup, reputedly clung to Trujillo "like a leech", and was appointed as the new Ambassador to the United States.

The customer selects the variety of sandwich from ten different categories divided up by protein, then checks the type of bread, chooses cheese and additional ingredients, and finally indicates the desired spreads and sauces. All sandwiches are sold at one price. In addition to the sandwiches, the chain sells hand-dipped shakes, chips, and freshly-baked cookies.

Between 1945 and 1948, the decision of the British government to halt Jewish immigration to the British Mandate of Palestine led to an increasingly bitter conflict between Britain and Palestinian Jews. In particular it resulted in large scale illegal Jewish immigration, "boat people", and Zionist political violence in Palestine. The policy of opposing Zionism led to deep divisions within the British leadership and Zionist violence provoked anti-semitism in Britain.

Mary: A Fiction is the only complete novel by 18th-century British feminist Mary Wollstonecraft. It tells the tragic story of a female's successive "romantic friendships" with a woman and a man. Composed while Wollstonecraft was a governess in Ireland, the novel was published in 1788 shortly after her summary dismissal and her decision to embark on a writing career, a precarious and disreputable profession for women in 18th-century Britain.

During their travels, Frisk learns the events leading up to the war between humans and monsters. Asriel, the son of Asgore and Toriel, had befriended a human child adopted by them. The child was fatally poisoned by flowers, and Asriel attempted to return the body to the humans, who attacked him instead. Mortally wounded, Asriel returned home to his parents and died, prompting Asgore to declare war.

On April 25, 2015, a tete-a-tete meeting was held between Ilham Aliyev and Francois Hollande, who was in Baku on a working visit. "The main topic was the Nagorno-Karabakh conflict, alongside discussions on regional cooperation, international governance, and the importance of UN reform and climate change."

Newsweek reported on alleged cult-like and abusive behavior in one controversial Washington DC AA group. One woman interviewed alleged pressure to have sex with senior members and to cut ties with outsiders and stop taking antidepressants. However, she reportedly found different practices in another AA group and noted these were not widespread and contrary to AA philosophy.

She was elected a member of the Prussian parliament ("Landtag") in 1921, representing an electoral district in East Prussia and dividing her time between Berlin and Königsberg. By this time she was a widow. Her political focus was on health, the arts, agriculture and questions involving farm workers. She also served as a member of the Prussian Regional Health Council. She continued to sit as a member of the Landtag till 1932, after which she settled in Osterode, to the south of Königsberg.

The engine of the 512 S was a completely new 60° V12 with 560 PS (404 kW) output. Compared to Porsche's air-cooled flat-12, it needed a maze of cooling pipes and a heavy radiator. Since the chassis was of sturded steel, reinforced with aluminium sheet, weight was 100 kg more than that of the alloy-framed 917. Notwithstanding the weight difference and higher center of gravity, the Ferrari 512 S and Porsche 917 seemed fairly evenly matched.

The Second Sudanese Civil War was a conflict from 1983 to 2005 between the Khartoum government and the Sudan People's Liberation Army. Originating in Bor, Malual-Chaat garrison under General Alier Nhial Mangardit, it later spread to the Nuba Mountains and Blue Nile.
The women's hockey tournament at the 2010 Commonwealth Games featured teams from 10 nations, each led by a head coach, with squad announcements made prior to the event.

Part of the ministry is for anti-abortion activism, mostly on the state side in front of abortion clinics. They hold peaceful protests where they stand in front of the clinics with signs and pray for the people going in and their fetuses. They also try to confront the mothers as they go in and convince them of alternatives to abortion. They also try to convince the groups who come through as to the importance of the issue.

Michael "Mike" Barnicle (born October 15, 1943) is an award-winning American social and political commentator and a print and broadcast journalist. He is periodic contributor and guest host on MSNBC's Morning Joe, Hardball with Chris Matthews and NBC's Today Show. He has been a regular contributor the local television news magazine, "Chronicle" since 1986. Barnicle has appeared on the PBS NewsHour, CBS's 60 Minutes, ESPN, and HBO sports programming.

Although the Autoharp is often thought of as a rhythm instrument for playing chordal accompaniment, modern players can play melodies on the instrument. Diatonic players are able to play fiddle tunes by using open-chording techniques, "pumping" the damper buttons while picking individual strings. Skilled chromatic players can perform a range of melodies.

The foundation stone of New Delhi was laid by George V during the Delhi Durbar of 1911. It was designed by British architects Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker. The new capital was inaugurated on 13 February 1931, by Viceroy and Governor-General Irwin.

Early primatologists had observed chimpanzees traveling through trees, eating fruits at their leisure, but rarely noted anything of interest in their behaviour. In the 1960s, there were only two field sites studying chimpanzee behaviour: one led by Jane Goodall in the Gombe Stream, and another located in the foothills of the Mahale Mountains at Lake Tanganyika, which was staffed by Japanese scientists and led by Nishida. Nishida's team planted sugarcane to attract the chimpanzees, who began to consistently visit the site after only six months. Based on his field observations, Nishida defended his dissertation at Kyoto University in 1968.

The Berlin Green Head is an ancient Egyptian statue head (AeMP 12500) made from greenschist and housed in the Egyptian Museum of Berlin, one floor down from the Nefertiti Bust in room 1.09. It has been considered the work of a highly skilled (though unknown) ancient Egyptian sculptor, as well as one of the most famous and credited pieces of art from the Late and Ptolemaic periods of ancient Egypt.

Some sources claim that Kimmel and Short were kept out of the information loop, allegedly on the orders of FDR, and that Edwin Layton was one of the individuals who suppressed information that should have reached Kimmel. Kimmel sought to locate the Japanese carriers, known to be at sea heading east, and launched a task force (described as an "exercise") on November 21 to attempt to intercept the Japanese fleet along the approach considered viable for an attack on Hawaii. Kimmel directed the task force to the location used by the Japanese on December 7.

Rašić married Leposava, the daughter of Gliša Isajlović, a telegraph operator and post office manager. They had three sons: Svetozar, Branivoje, and Dragoljub and three daughters: Katarina, Ružica, and Jelena. In 1509, aged 18, Íñigo took up arms for Antonio Manrique de Lara, 2nd Duke of Nájera. His diplomacy and leadership qualities earned him the title "servant of the court", and made him very useful to the Duke. Under the Duke's leadership, Íñigo participated in many battles without injury. However at the Battle of Pamplona on 20 May 1521 he was gravely injured when a French-Navarrese expedition force stormed the fortress of Pamplona, and a cannonball ricocheting off a nearby wall shattered his right leg. Íñigo was returned to his father's castle in Loyola, where, in an era before anesthetics, he underwent several surgical operations to repair the leg, with his bones set and rebroken. In the end, the operations left his right leg shorter than the other. He would limp for the rest of his life, with his military career over.

Franklin's main interest was the relationship between a patient's environment and mental illness. She developed planned environmental therapy to treat this relationship. She theorised that focusing on the well-functioning parts of a patient’s personality could help them to behave better in a social environment. Planned environmental therapy is seen as a forerunner of the therapeutic community movement.

Tales of Zestiria the X (テイルズ オブ ゼスティリア ザ X, Teiruzu Obu Zesutiria Za Kurosu, pronounced "Tales of Zestiria the Cross") is a Japanese anime television series based on both Tales of Zestiria and Tales of Berseria video games developed by Bandai Namco Studios and tri-Crescendo, and published by Bandai Namco Entertainment. It is produced by Ufotable, directed by Haruo Sotozaki and written by Hikaru Kondo, featuring character designs by Akira Matsushima and music by Motoi Sakuraba and Go Shiina. The anime series is split into two halves: the first half premiered on July 3, 2016 and finished airing on September 25, 2016. The second half premiered on January 8, 2017 and finished airing on April 29, 2017.

Lake Aloha is a feature of the wilderness area, with shallow, clear waters sitting in a wide granite basin carved by glaciers in the last ice age. Despite trade rumors during the 2013 offseason, Aldridge voiced his desire to stay put in Portland, while also asking for improvements to the Blazers' roster. Aldridge started out his eighth NBA season strong, recording five straight double-doubles from November 9 to 17. On November 23, 2013, in a game against the Warriors, Aldridge was involved in an altercation after Warriors center Andrew Bogut became entangled with Joel Freeland. On November 25, it was announced that he was fined $45,000 for escalating the incident. The very same day, Aldridge was named Western Conference Player of the Week for the fourth time in his career. The award came in the midst of an 11-game win streak for the Blazers, during which Aldridge averaged 21.1 points, 11.3 rebounds, 2.5 assists, and 2.5 blocks per game. On December 12, 2013, Aldridge scored 31 points and grabbed a career-high 25 rebounds in a 111–104 victory over the Houston Rockets, becoming the first player in Portland Trail Blazers franchise history with at least 30 points and 25 rebounds in a game. Though Christianity in Africa was largely a European import that arrived with colonialism, but this is not the case with the Tigrinya people. The ancient Kingdom of Axum centered in what is now northern Ethiopia (or Tigray) had intimate connections with the Mediterranean world in which Christianity grew. Christianity arrived in the Horn of Africa in the fourth century, growing dynamically in the pre-existing Jewish/Animistic mixed environment. The Tigrinya people thus converted to Christianity centuries before most of Europe, thereby establishing one of the oldest state churches in the world.

The wing was braced to the lower fuselage on each side with V-struts to the spars and the central-section joined to the upper fuselage on steel cabane struts. It describes, in a visual (cause-and-effect network) diagram, the main perceived symptoms (along with secondary or hidden ones that lead up to the perceived symptoms) of a problem scenario and ultimately the apparent root causes or core conflict. The benefit of building a CRT is that it identifies the connections or dependencies between perceived symptoms (effects) and root causes (core problems or conflicts) explicitly. If core problems are identified, prioritized, and tackled well, multiple undesirable effects in the system will disappear. Leaders may then focus on solving the few core problems which would cause the biggest positive systemic changes.

The giant thicket rat (*Grammomys gigas*), endemic to Mount Kenya's tropical forests and high-altitude shrublands, is endangered due to habitat loss from deforestation for timber, firewood, and agricultural conversion. Climate change further threatens its survival. Since 1996, IUCN has classified it as endangered, citing declining populations and restricted range. Limited data exist on its abundance, but research priorities include studying its distribution, taxonomy, and threats. Much of its range lies in rapidly degrading forests, making habitat protection a conservation priority. IUCN recommends further research on population trends, ecology, and threats, while proposed projects aim to assess elevation-based distribution and abundance using methods like Spatially Explicit Capture Recapture (SECR) to inform conservation strategies.

Martin Schütz, a theoretical chemist, focused on developing *ab initio* quantum chemical methods, particularly for accurate electronic structure calculations. At the University of Stuttgart, he advanced explicitly correlated coupled-cluster (CC) methods, such as CCSD(T)-F12, building on Hans-Joachim Werner's 1980s work. His early-2000s research invoked local correlation techniques and orbital-specific virtual (OSV) approaches, extending triple substitutions to larger molecules. Previous methods were limited to small systems due to computational expense, but Schütz's innovations improved scalability. His work addressed limitations in describing excited states and photochemical processes, as seen in collaborations on photolyase repair mechanisms and triplet-state calculations.

In the Assyrian community in the Jazira Region, opposition was expressed regarding the measure, as the community is significantly affected due to high real estate ownership and a notable share of outbound civil war refugees. William Methwold (1590 – 5 March 1653) was an English merchant and agent of the East India Company. He traveled extensively in India, and in 1622, became the first Englishman to visit the Golconda diamond mines. His account of his travels was published in 1626. He served as president of Surat and negotiated the Goa Convention with the Portuguese in 1635. Methwold was elected deputy governor of the East India Company in 1643, and re-elected annually until his death.

Victory Day over Nazism in World War II (Ukrainian: День перемоги над нацизмом у Другій світовій війні) is a Ukrainian holiday celebrated annually on May 9. Established in 2015, it replaced the Soviet-era Victory Day, following the country's decommunization laws. The holiday aims to shift the focus from glorifying war to honoring personal histories, similar to Remembrance Day. The remembrance poppy is the official symbol.

The La Salle Explorers women's basketball team, as of the 2015–16 season, held an overall record of 3.37 GPA-academic excellence. Nassau County Route 8 is an unsigned county road in Nassau County, New York. It travels between Old Country Road (CR 25) and Rockaway Avenue (CR E06) in Garden City and Northern Boulevard (NY 25A) in Manhasset.

Nassau County Route 8 travels along Herricks Road and Shelter Rock Road, is roughly five miles (8.0 km) in length, and is maintained by the Nassau County Department of Public Works.

The Hiller YH-32 Hornet was a jet-powered helicopter designed in the 1950s as an experimental aircraft. It featured ramjets on its rotor tips, aiming to eliminate the need for a tail rotor. However, the design faced issues with high fuel consumption, poor range, and noise, limiting its practical application. Evaluated by both the U.S. Army and Navy, the YH-32 showed potential as an armed helicopter and for air rescue and reconnaissance roles but ultimately failed to gain widespread adoption due to its limitations. Only a few prototypes were built, and the project was eventually canceled.

In 1922, the Davies court ruled that the criminal provisions of the CGA fell without the ambit of the "in any case whatever" section 36 provisions of the Supreme Court Act of 1906, and that therefore the legislators could and did draw up a statute which limited the power of the Supreme Court of Canada (SCC). The defendant, a Manitoba elevator, was convicted for selling grain on commission without a licence, in contravention of section 215 of the Canada Grain Act of 1912. His conviction was quashed by the Court of Appeal, which held section 215 of the Grain Act to be ultra vires of the Dominion Parliament, and the prosecution was handcuffed by the concurrence of highest court.

The return of Napoleon restored cooperation between the members of the Quadruple Alliance. Britain and Austria feared renewed French expansion, strengthening Prussia’s position after Waterloo. The Prussians pressed for territorial gains, securing 40% of Saxony, Swedish Pomerania, the Rhineland, Westphalia, and parts of Poland. Prussia also acquired the economically vital Rhine Province and Royal Prussia, consolidating its dominance in northern Germany. These acquisitions, alongside Swedish Pomerania and Westphalia, expanded Prussian influence, countering French ambitions and reshaping Central Europe’s balance of power.

As a Centre party Member of Parliament between 1920 and 1933, Georg Schreiber's focus was on cultural issues, broadly defined. He sought to improve collaboration between religious and worldly elements. Naturally he also represented church interests. He was an influential member of the parliamentary budget committee. At the same time he tried to reduce what he saw as an unnecessary distancing between Centre Party and the universities establishment, to stand up for the interests of "intellectual and spiritual workers", and to reconcile the traditionalist church community with the new republican order.

USS *Housatonic* (SP-1697) was a freighter launched in 1899 as *El Rio* by Newport News Shipbuilding for the Morgan Line. Originally serving as a commercial vessel between New York and Gulf Coast ports, it was acquired by the U.S. Navy in 1917 during World War I and converted into a minelayer. Commissioned on January 25, 1918, *Housatonic* laid 9,339 mines as part of the North Sea Mine Barrage and later transported American troops home after the war. Decommissioned in 1919, it returned to commercial service, renamed *Brazos* in 1925. The ship was lost in a collision with HMS *Archer* on January 13, 1942, during World War II.

Equinox Minerals was formed in January 2004 through a merger with Equinox Resources and was incorporated as a Canadian company in the same month. The company began its operations in Zambia in 1996, where it acquired the Lumwana project in 1999, a site that had seen at least ten previous companies fail to develop it. The Lumwana project encompasses two major copper mines and covers an area of 1,355 square kilometers. In 2010, Equinox also acquired the Jabal Sayid project, located approximately 350 kilometers from Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, which was expected to have an annual ore production capacity of 60,000 tonnes starting in 2012. In February 2011, Equinox launched a hostile takeover bid valued at $4.8 billion for Lundin Mining, which was simultaneously attempting to merge with Inmet Mining. In April 2011, Minmetals Resources made a takeover offer for Equinox, which was rejected, while Equinox accepted a bid from Barrick Gold Corporation, announced on April 11, 2011, for 7.2 billion Canadian dollars ($7.4 billion).

Mordaunt Fenwick-Bisset (died July 7, 1884) was an Irish landowner and politician who served as a Member of Parliament for West Somerset from 1880 until his resignation in 1883. He was also a prominent figure in staghunting, serving as Master of the Devon and Somerset Staghounds from 1855 to 1881, where he played a key role in revitalizing the sport and increasing wild red deer populations on Exmoor.

Another six years passed before the couple's next filmed screenplay, this time for the romantic comedy Murphy's Romance (1985), based on a novel by Max Schott. The Foreign Affairs Ministry expresses concerns over the illegality and danger of the referendum announced for Crimea on March 16, 2014. MFA considers this referendum illegal and not compliant with the Constitution of Ukraine and international law, and thus cannot serve as a basis for any change in Crimea's status. Albania urges the Russian Federation to cease supporting this dangerous, destabilizing initiative that violates Ukraine's sovereignty, territorial integrity, and unity, and strongly condemns any comparison between the declaration of independence of Crimea and that of Kosovo. The use of the Advisory Opinion of the International Court of Justice on Kosovo's independence to justify the legality of the Crimean referendum lacks legal basis and does not reflect historical and factual circumstances. Following the referendum, the Ministry aligns with the international community's stance that this referendum sets a dangerous precedent for order and international relations, and that any solution to the Ukraine crisis must respect Ukraine's territorial integrity and sovereignty, as well as international law.
Laura Rajnović Evetović (Serbian Cyrillic: Лаура Рајновић Еветовић; born 1 June 1975) is a politician and administrator in Serbia. She served in the Assembly of Vojvodina from 2012 to 2020.

Facebook, Inc. v. Duguid, 592 U.S. ___ (2021), was a United States Supreme Court case related to the definition and function of auto dialers under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991 (TCPA) to send unsolicited text messages. In a unanimous decision based on statutory interpretation of the TCPA, the Supreme Court ruled that auto dialers are defined by their function to either store or produce telephone numbers from a random or sequential number generator.

In early 2009, Hurley took over as the co-presenter of Sunday Sport alongside Con Murphy on RTÉ Radio 1. "At the age of 25, she made history as the first ever female presenter in the show's 40-year history and writes the 'Jacqui's Week' column."

Jabłonna is a village in Legionowo County, Masovian Voivodeship, Poland, with a population of 7,365. It is known for the Jabłonna Palace, a baroque palace built in the late 18th century and now a hotel and event center owned by the Polish Academy of Sciences. The village also contains the Mother of God Queen of Poland Church, originally built in the 1920s and rebuilt after being destroyed in World War II.

According to Blond, these two large-scale realities, while usually spoken of as diametrically opposed, are in reality the two sides of the same coin. He argues that modern and postmodern individualism and statism have been intertwined since Hobbes, with Tocqueville and Nisbet also analyzing this link. In *The Guardian* and *The Independent*, Blond advocates for civic conservatism, emphasizing localism and mutualism. He praises a new Tory settlement for rejecting hyper-liberalism and fostering community, believing it could dismantle the bureaucratic-capitalist alliance and restore social cohesion.

Bipolar disorder (BD) is a mood disorder usually characterized by extreme mood swings and oscillating periods of intense emotion, such as mania and depression. The cycling between manic and depressive episodes is the main hallmark of bipolar disorder. In BD, there seems to be a disconnect between the Frontoparietal Network (FPN) and Defualt Mode Network (DMN), in which the FPN is unable "to suppress task irrelevant DMN activity during cognitive performance" which leads to BD's cognitive impairments. It also appears that in BD, there is a disruption in the recruitment of the salience network which contributes to cognitive dysregulation. Bipolar disorder is also thought to be characterized by abnormal functional connectivity between the FPN and motivational networks, with the DMN playing a mediation role. All these aberrant connectivity and dysregulation leads to the BD pathology witnessed in patients with this mood disorder.

The 2012 film *Heroine* opened with ₹7.5 crore on its first day and grossed ₹25 crore nett in its opening weekend (Fri: ₹7.5 cr, Sat: ₹8.5 cr, Sun: ₹9 cr). However, its collections dropped sharply by 50% on Monday, earning ₹3.75 crore. By the end of its first week, the film had accumulated approximately ₹40 crore nett. Total lifetime collections reached around ₹48 crore nett in India.

Rentenbank’s special promotional loans are geared towards businesses in the agricultural, horticultural, viticultural and silvicultural sectors, including manufacturers of agricultural input materials as well as agriculture-related trade and service companies. The bank also finances projects in the food industry and the associated upstream and downstream industries. Further, Rentenbank promotes civic engagement and public-sector investments in rural areas. The bank’s statutory promotional mandate explicitly includes promoting renewable energy, renewable agricultural resources, organic farming, agriculture-related environmental and consumer protection, and animal welfare. The loans are extended via local banks on a competitively neutral basis. In addition, Rentenbank awards grants for innovations and applied research projects, as well as for projects and institutions that are of particular importance to agribusiness and rural areas. Rentenbank’s Rehwinkel Foundation supports scientific research, promotes events that foster collaboration between agricultural science and industry, and awards scholarships to students on relevant Master’s programmes. Rentenbank focuses its efforts on promoting innovation, in particular start-ups that are developing new, tech-based business ideas that can play a vital role in driving innovation in agriculture.

"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, written in 1793 during a summer vacation in Devon. Inspired by local folklore and the Pixies' Parlour near Ottery, the poem blends themes of fairies, mythology, and poetic self-reflection. It was first published in Coleridge's 1796 *Poems on Various Subjects* but later dismissed by the poet himself. The work draws on influences from Milton, Shakespeare, and other literary figures, showcasing Coleridge's early imaginative style. While receiving mixed critical responses, it is noted for its inventive imagery and its reflection of Coleridge's evolving poetic identity.

The organization collaborates with federal and state environmental agencies, Congress, state legislatures, and the courts to address issues such as global warming, pollution, and energy efficiency, while promoting the conservation of natural resources and the environment. It participates in litigation in federal and state courts to support the implementation and enforcement of the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, and other environmental laws. The organization also supports an environmental science program involving staff and associated scientists, which includes efforts to promote more sustainable manufacturing practices and public education initiatives.

'The Mark of the Angels – Miserere' was widely released in France on February 6, 2013, initially showing in 200 theaters. In its second week, the number dropped to 150 theaters. The film is expected to premiere in Belgium later in 2013, with sales handled by Wild Bunch.

Thorstein Veblen's socio-economic theories were influenced by thinkers including Charles Darwin, Karl Marx, Adam Smith, and Herbert Spencer, emphasizing evolutionary development in human institutions. Veblen critiqued 19th-century economic theories as static and hedonistic, arguing that economists should consider social and cultural behaviors rather than relying solely on theoretical abstractions. While neoclassical economics models individuals as rational utility-maximizers, Veblen proposed that economic behavior is often driven by social status and class position rather than pure rationality or happiness maximization. His institutional economics approach contrasted with prevailing economic models of his time by focusing on the social and cultural dimensions of economic activity.

He married one of the original Fisk Jubilee Singers, Georgia Gordon Taylor (1855–1913), better known as Georgia Taylor. She was the daughter of George Gordon, who was a slave, and a mulatto mother, Mercy Duke Gordon. At the age of 18, Georgia joined the group of vocalists from Fisk University, touring the U.S. and Europe in 1872 and 1873. After returning, she married Taylor, and they had a son in 1891 who died in infancy. Georgia continued singing until her death in 1913, remaining Taylor's closest companion. Following her passing, Taylor married Ida D. Mallory.

The T-V distinction marks varying levels of formality using different pronouns. In contrast, languages with pronoun avoidance, such as Thai, Burmese, and Vietnamese, do not use pronouns for politeness; instead, they employ status and kinship terms, titles, or avoid pronouns entirely. Second-person pronouns in these languages are mainly reserved for close equals, family members, and inferiors.
Deitingen is a municipality in the district of Wasseramt, in the canton of Solothurn, Switzerland.

The Empire of Atlantium is a social and political advocacy group based in Sydney, Australia, that claims to be a sovereign state, though it is not recognized as such by any established nation. It was established, in 1981, by a group of three teenagers, and now claims approximately 1,000 members, in over 60 countries. George Francis Cruickshank acts as head of state with the title "Emperor George II".
Aberdare Girls' School was a secondary school in Aberdare, Wales, established in 1913 and closed in 2014. It merged with two other schools to form Aberdare Community School.

In 1963, Lieutenant William Szili, who served as the executive officer of a Marine company responsible for security at the Guantanamo Bay Naval Base, provided his account of the 1961 shooting of Rubén López Sabariego. The company was commanded by Captain Arthur J. Jackson, a Medal of Honor recipient for his heroic actions during World War II at Peleliu. Szili recounted that on the night of the incident, October 18, 1961, he and Jackson had consumed several martini cocktails at the base officer's club. Szili was later called by base police, who informed him that Jackson had found López, a Cuban employee, in a restricted area near the Lee Ward Point Magazine.

Johnson & Wales Business School was founded in September 1914 in Providence, Rhode Island. Founders Gertrude I. Johnson and Mary T. Wales met as students at Pennsylvania State Normal School in Millersville, Pennsylvania. Years later, both were teaching at Bryant and Stratton business school in Providence (now Bryant University) when they decided to team up and open a business school. The school opened with one student and one typewriter on Hope Street in Providence. The school soon moved to a larger site on Olney Street, and later moved downtown to 36 Exchange Street to better serve returning soldiers after World War I. The curriculum in the early part of the 20th century included bookkeeping, typing, shorthand, English, and mathematics. The school admitted both men and women.

Matilda of Tuscany (1046 – 24 July 1115), sometimes referred to as "the Great Countess" or Matilda of Canossa after her ancestral castle, was a member of the House of Canossa (also known as the Attonids) in the second half of the eleventh century. Matilda is recognized as a significant figure of the Italian Middle Ages. She lived during a period marked by battles, intrigues, and excommunications, and is noted for her leadership abilities in challenging circumstances.

Mongolia competed at the 2004 Summer Olympics in Athens, Greece, from 13 to 29 August 2004. This was the nation's tenth appearance at the Olympics, except the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, because of its partial support to the Soviet boycott.

The Mongolian National Olympic Committee sent a total of 20 athletes, 13 men and 7 women, to compete in 7 different sports, tying its delegation count with Sydney four years earlier. In the wake of Munkhbayar Dorjsuren's transfer to the German team, three Mongolian athletes had previously competed in Sydney, including shooter Otryadyn Gündegmaa in the women's pistol events. Judoka and world champion Damdinsürengiin Nyamkhüü was appointed by the committee to become Mongolia's flag bearer in the opening ceremony, dressed in a traditional costume.

The London Ultimate Club (LUC), founded in 1994 and incorporated as a not-for-profit in 2008, promotes ultimate frisbee through summer leagues and indoor leagues during the fall and winter seasons in London, Ontario.

The Eastern Bloc dominated the 1976 Montreal Olympics, with seven countries placing in the top ten of the medal table. The United States team was relegated to a third place in the medal standings for the first time in its history. This was an Olympics of contrasts: the US men's swimming team, despite the generally dismal showing of the overall delegation, swept 12 gold and 27 total medals in the 13 events that were on the program and broke 11 world records in the process (arguably the most dominant performance of any swimming team in history), while the US women's swimming team, on the other hand, fell victim to what was later shown to be a pervasive East German doping program. They still managed to win a gold medal, in an upset of the East Germans in the 4x100 freestyle relay. The event was held on the last day of the swimming program, and the American women were risking being deprived of gold for the first time in US Olympic history. The victory was somewhat overlooked at the time, but since the early 1990s, when public revelation of the doping program began, their gold medal is considered to be one of the most improbable upsets of all time. In track and field, both the US men's and women's team were overwhelmed by East Germans who secured a bulk of medals in the signature sports of the US, resulting in the USSR topping the medal table. The US boxing team surprised everyone, advancing to six gold medal bouts and winning five of them, drawing parallels to a stellar 1952 team that also took five golds. The achievement was even more notable due to the fact that the American boxers were significantly younger and less experienced than their Cuban and Soviet counterparts. In other sports, US divers won five medals, including two golds; the US equestrian team took home four medals; American shooters won three medals, including a historic silver by a woman in the mixed 50 meters rifle three positions; US freestyle wrestlers advanced to four gold medal bouts, yet won only one of them (all four were against the Soviets), concluding the meet with six medals overall.

Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche (October 15, 1844–August 25, 1900), a German philologist and philosopher, produced critiques of contemporary culture, religion, and philosophy centered around the positive and negative attitudes of various systems of morality toward life, especially within the context of his anti-Christian viewpoint. Beyond the controversial themes in his works, Nietzsche's powerful literary style and subtle approach distinguish his writings. Although largely overlooked during his short yet astonishingly productive working life, which ended with a mental collapse in 1889, Nietzsche received recognition in German intellectual circles during the first half of the 20th century, and as a highly significant figure in modern philosophy in the second half of the 20th century.

The Tithwal Bridge is a crossing point over the Kishanganga River on the Line of Control (LoC). First constructed in 1931, it was destroyed during the Indo-Pakistan War of 1947. The bridge was reconstructed in 1988. However, context does not confirm which countries jointly reconstructed the bridge. The bridge connects Tithwal in India and Neelum in Pakistan.

Local councils in Israel are municipal units designated for settlements with populations exceeding 2,000, but not large enough to be classified as cities. The Israeli Interior Minister determines the status of these councils, considering residents' preferences regarding their classification. Local councils play a significant role in town planning and are represented by the Union of Local Authorities in Israel (ULAI), established in 1938 to advocate for local councils at the national level.

Tales of the Heroes: Twin Brave is an action game in the Tales series of games. published by Bandai Namco Games. It is considered a spinoff, and plays similarly to the Dynasty Warriors series. The game was released for the PlayStation Portable in Japan on February 23, 2012.

The Limestone Coast is a region in southeastern South Australia, known for its unique geology, wine production, and coastal landscapes. It includes towns such as Mount Gambier, Naracoorte, and Robe, as well as significant wetlands and the World Heritage-listed Naracoorte Caves. The area features Mediterranean climate, viticulture, and diverse wildlife, though much of its natural habitat has been altered by agriculture.

*The Gateway* (originally titled *Curtains*) is a 2015 horror film that follows Danni, a former hospice nurse turned whale conservation activist, who discovers a mysterious portal in her shower. Her investigation leads to deadly consequences, including encounters with monsters and a sinister figure known as the Pale Man. The film received mixed reviews, with critics praising its originality and charm but noting its limited appeal and unconventional narrative.

Give It Up is the second studio album by American musician Bonnie Raitt. Released in 1972 by Warner Bros. Records, Give It Up is an amalgamation of various genres, including folk, blues, R&B, and soft rock. Seven of the ten tracks on the album are covers, and the tracks are either soft sentimental ballads or fast-paced folk rock pieces. Lyrically, Give It Up revolves around Raitt's femininity, relationships, and being comfortable with herself. Raitt recorded the album at Bearsville Studios with producer Michael Cuscuna.

Give It Up was Raitt's first album to reach the Billboard Top LPs & Tapes chart, where it peaked at number 138. The initial reception was positive, as critics liked the vocals and the musicianship throughout the album. Contemporary reviews are also positive, with some critics calling Give It Up a stand-out album within Raitt's discography. In 2012, Rolling Stone ranked Give It Up at number 495 on its list of the 500 Greatest Albums of All Time.

The Joseph Rowntree Foundation has proposed replacing the current Council Tax system, based on bands of house prices, with a system more closely related to property values. This proposal suggests it could increase taxes on higher-value properties and decrease them on lower-value ones, potentially reducing house price volatility.

Marahoué National Park is a national park in central Ivory Coast, established in 1968. It originally contained a diversity of habitats, including dense forest and savanna. Illegal logging and settling have resulted in almost complete deforestation and conversion to agricultural land. The park was once home to chimpanzees, but they are now extirpated.

The Utría National Natural Park (Lang-es) is a national park in the Chocó Department, Colombia. It contains diverse flora and fauna in a lush, mountainous rainforest environment with some of the highest rainfall in the world, at up to 10,000 millimetres (390 in) annually. The park also protects the coastal marine environment, and is known for visits by humpback whales, who give birth in the lagoon after which the park is named, and sea turtles who nest on the beaches. There is accommodation for visitors, and ecotourism services are provided by the local indigenous people and members of the coastal Afro-Colombian communities.

The Purpose Driven Network connects over 400,000 Christian congregations from more than 200 denominations. The Grand Loop Road is a historic roadway in Yellowstone National Park, Wyoming, that forms a figure-eight loop connecting many of the park's most notable natural features and landmarks. Constructed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and later maintained by the National Park Service, the road was added to the National Register of Historic Places on December 23, 2003. 

The route begins near Mammoth Hot Springs at the park's headquarters and traverses diverse landscapes, including Golden Gate Canyon, Obsidian Canyon, and the Norris Geyser Basin. It passes iconic sites such as Sheepeater Cliff, Old Faithful in the Upper Geyser Basin, Yellowstone Lake, and the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone. The road also crosses the Continental Divide and Dunraven Pass, offering access to geysers, waterfalls, and scenic valleys. 

The Grand Loop Road connects with several entrance roads, providing access to neighboring areas like Grand Teton National Park and Cooke City, Montana, making it a central feature of Yellowstone's infrastructure.

*The Fosters* was originally conceived by openly gay creators Bradley Bredeweg and Peter Paige, who sought to write a family drama reflecting the "modern American family." Initially considering a story about two gay fathers, they concluded the subject had been explored well in shows like *Modern Family* and *The New Normal*. Instead, they shifted focus to two women raising a family, recognizing a lack of such narratives on television. Bredeweg noted, "There was a kind of a vacuum when it came to stories about women raising families," drawing inspiration from their friends—mothers raising biological, fostered, or adopted children. The foster care element was influenced by Bredeweg's childhood friend, who endured multiple placements before being adopted in high school, as well as Paige's research on LGBT youth in foster care. This blend of personal and observed experiences shaped the series' unique premise.

Han Lao Da (born Ann Jong Juan, October 22, 1947) is a Singaporean xiangsheng performer, playwright, and the principal of Han Language Centre. He began composing xiangsheng plays in the 1970s and has since worked to promote the art form in Singapore, publishing several collections of scripts.

Eblana (Lang-el) is an ancient Irish settlement that appears in the Geographia of Claudius Ptolemaeus (Ptolemy), the Greek astronomer and cartographer, around the year 140 AD. It was traditionally believed by scholars to refer to the same site as the modern city of Dublin. The 19th-century writer Louis Agassiz used Eblana as a Latin equivalent for Dublin. However, more recent scholarship favours the north County Dublin seaside village of Loughshinny due to its proximity to Drumanagh, an important trading site with strong links to Roman Britain; it has even been described as a bridgehead of a possible Roman invasion. However, there is no definitive proof to tie Eblana with any location, so its exact identity is still a matter of speculation.

Nathan Read, born in 1759, experimented with steam-powered transportation in the late 18th century. In 1790, he made several models of a steamcar and a steamboat, aiming to improve navigation and transportation. His steamboat used a paddle-wheel arrangement, while he designed a portable high-pressure steam engine for propulsion. In 1806, he established a shipbuilding factory with friends to further develop his steamboat designs. Evidence suggests he created a multi-tubular boiler and secured patents for his innovations, though a full-scale steamcar model remains undocumented.

Psychoanalyst Sigmund Freud made the concept of the unconscious popular; and he based most of his theories on psychoanalysis on the concept. Freud described the subconscious (or preconscious) as just below consciousness, accessible with effort, while the unconscious held repressed, traumatic content. He believed unconscious emotions were projected onto others and accessed via dreams or slips. Unlike the subconscious, the unconscious remained hidden, influencing behavior without direct awareness. Pierre Janet coined "subconscious."

The Arnot Art Museum is a historic art museum in Elmira, New York, housed in a Greek Revival mansion built in 1833. Founded by banker Matthias H. Arnot, it features 19th-century European and American art, including works by Bouguereau, Bierstadt, Cole, and Millet. The museum expanded in 1982 and remains a contributing property to the Elmira Civic Historic District.

Nazi Germany ended the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact of August 1939 by invading the Soviet Union in Operation Barbarossa on June 22, 1941, shifting to a strategic defensive posture on the Western Front and opening an Eastern Front that would lead to Germany's eventual defeat. Slovakia and Romania supported Nazi Germany from the outset, followed by Hungary, Italy, and Finland joining the invasion. Ultimately, many European nations contributed troops to the Eastern Front. Following the invasion, the territories previously gained by the Soviet Union under the pact were quickly lost, and countries like the Baltic states became German protectorates, providing forces to support Nazi Germany.

On 5 November 1986, the base camp was officially named Camp Gonsalves, in memory of PFC Harold Gonsalves who was posthumously awarded the Medal of Honor for his heroic actions during the Battle of Okinawa. In March 1998, to better convey NTA as a training base, the name was officially changed to the Marine Corps Jungle Warfare Training Center, the only existing Department of Defense jungle training facility for Marine and Joint Forces. As of March 2000 the area had 71 landowners, with a yearly rental fee of 476 million yen and a total of three Japanese employees.

The Apostles' Fast is a Christian fasting period observed by various Orthodox traditions, beginning on the Monday after All Saints' Sunday and lasting until June 29, the Feast of the Holy Apostles Peter and Paul. Instituted as a preparation for the apostles' missionary work following the Resurrection of Jesus, the Fast is rooted in scriptural references where Jesus speaks of the need for fasting after his departure. While not as strict as other fasting periods, it typically involves abstaining from red meat, poultry, dairy, and eggs, with some variations among different Orthodox communities. The duration of the Fast can range from eight to 42 days, depending on the date of Pascha (Easter).

A two-stage design (separate charging section ahead of the collecting section) has the benefit of minimizing ozone production, which would adversely affect the health of personnel working in enclosed spaces. For shipboard engine rooms where gearboxes generate an oil mist, two-stage ESP's are used to clean the air, improving the operating environment and preventing buildup of flammable oil fog accumulations. Collected oil is returned to the gear lubricating system.

The 2010 conservatism-psychoticism correlation error refers to a mistake in a study by Brad Verhulst, Peter K. Hatemi, and Nicholas G. Martin, which incorrectly reported positive correlations between psychoticism and conservative attitudes. The error, later repeated in subsequent publications, reversed the actual negative correlations. While the mistake did not affect the study's primary thesis, it caused confusion in later research and public discourse. The error was eventually corrected after scrutiny by researchers, sparking debates over data transparency and the interpretation of psychoticism in political psychology.

The Latter Day Saints began migrating to Missouri after Smith identified the area as the New Jerusalem. They also occupied the Kirtland area and the Independence area for about seven years. After Smith was forced out of Kirtland in 1838, Latter Day Saints from Kirtland moved to Missouri, increasing their numbers there and raising concerns among local leaders and residents about the influence of the Latter Day Saints.
Tome VI is a 1968 jazz album by Gil Mellé, released on Verve Records. It features electronic effects and a revamped quartet, marking a technical breakthrough in live electronic jazz performances.

Retaj Hotels & Hospitality is a Qatar-based hotel and hospitality company owned by five Qatari investors. The company has expanded internationally, with properties in Turkey, Saudi Arabia, and the Comoros, and plans to enter the European and U.S. markets. Originally created by Matthew Mercer for his personal campaigns in 2012, Exandria is the world where the Critical Role web series is set and is a high fantasy setting which moves away from the "harmful tropes in D&D" around monstrous races. In 2017, Green Ronin Publishing released a setting book written by Mercer and James Haeck, Critical Role: Tal'Dorei Campaign Setting, which focused on the continent of Tal'Dorei on Exandria, where the first Critical Role campaign with the adventuring group Vox Machina took place. One critic highlighted that this campaign has the "specific flavor of high fantasy drama" akin to the "Dragonlance or Forgotten Realms books". An updated edition, titled Tal'Dorei Campaign Setting Reborn (2022) from publisher Darrington Press, moves the timeline 20 years forward to the era of the second Critical Role campaign.

Eigenmannia and other weakly electric fish use active electrolocation – they can locate objects by generating an electric field and detecting distortions in the field caused by interference from those objects. Electric fish use their electric organ to create electric fields, and they detect small distortions of these fields using special electroreceptive organs in the skin. All fish with the JAR are wave-discharging fish that emit steady quasi-sinusoidal discharges. For the genus Eigenmannia, frequencies range from 240 to 600 Hz. The EOD frequency is very steady, typically with less than 0.3% variation over a 10-minute time span.

The Mouassine Mosque, also known as La Mosquee Mouassine, is a significant historical mosque located in the Mouassine district in the heart of the Medina of Marrakesh. Constructed between 1562-1563 AD (970 AH) and 1572-1573 AD (980 AH) by the Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib, the mosque is part of a larger complex. This complex includes a library, a hammam, a midha (ablutions facility), and a former children's Qur'anic school (msid). The Mouassine Fountain, located just east of the mosque, is one of the city's finest public fountains and a landmark in the Medina. It features a carved wood awning and filigreed iron grill. The Mouassine Fountain is one of the largest in Marrakesh, along with the fountain of the Bab Doukkala Mosque and the Shrob ou Shouf Fountain, all from the Saadian period.
