Acoustic torpedo  

An acoustic torpedo is a self-guided underwater missile that uses sonar to detect and home in on a target, typically a ship or submarine. These torpedoes employ passive sonar to detect noise from the target (such as engine or propeller sounds) or active sonar to reflect pulses off the target. Early models had fixed sensor patterns, while modern versions use computer-controlled systems for greater precision.  

First deployed in World War II, the German G7es (T5) torpedo proved highly effective against Allied ships, forcing the development of countermeasures like the Foxer noisemaker, which created distracting cavitation noise to lure torpedoes away. Allied tactics, such as the "Step-Aside" maneuver, also reduced their effectiveness. Postwar advancements led to improved decoy systems like the AN/SLQ-25 Nixie and AN/SLQ-61 Lightweight Tow.  

Captured German technology, including from U-505 and U-250, provided valuable insights into acoustic torpedo design. Despite initial setbacks, countermeasures significantly reduced their wartime success rate, with only about 77 out of 700 German torpedoes hitting their targets. Today, acoustic torpedoes remain a key component of naval warfare, with ongoing improvements in guidance and evasion tactics.

The Sugar Loaf Islands, known as Ngā Motu in Māori, are a group of islands located near Port Taranaki, New Zealand. The islands are divided into inner and outer groups, with the inner islands including Mataora (Round Rock), Pararaki (Seagull Rock), and Motuotamatea (Snapper Rock), which are connected to the mainland at low tides. The outer islands consist of Motumahanga (Saddleback Island) and Moturoa. Historically significant, Mikotahi was once a half-tide island and the site of the first oil well in the Commonwealth. The islands have served as hunting and gathering grounds for local Māori tribes for centuries. In 1986, the area was designated as a marine park, later reinforced by the Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Park Act in 1991, which protects the islands and surrounding waters from commercial activities. The islands are also notable for their geological significance, representing some of the oldest volcanic activity in the Taranaki region.

Buddhist tradition recounts that in 592, Guan Yu appeared before Master Zhiyi, founder of the Tientai school, during meditation on Yuquan Hill (Jade Stream Mountain). Accounts describe Guan Yu arriving with spiritual attendants and requesting Buddhist teachings. After receiving instruction in the Dharma, Guan Yu is said to have taken refuge in the triple gems and accepted the Five Precepts. John Thomas Gordon Ruddy (born 24 October 1986) is an English professional footballer who plays as a goalkeeper for Championship club Birmingham City.

Ruddy began his career at Cambridge United, making his professional debut in 2004. In 2005, he moved to Everton, where he made only one senior appearance, spending much of his time on loan at various clubs, including Walsall, Rushden & Diamonds, Chester City, Stockport County, Wrexham, Bristol City and Crewe Alexandra. In 2009, he joined Motherwell on loan, where he made 34 appearances.

In 2010, Ruddy signed for Norwich City, where he became a first-team regular and helped the club gain promotion to the Premier League. He was voted the club's Player's Player of the Season in 2012 and earned a call-up to the England squad for UEFA Euro 2012. After Norwich were relegated from the Premier League, Ruddy stayed with the club and helped them bounce back via the play-offs.

In 2017, Ruddy joined Wolverhampton Wanderers, where he won the Championship title and was named in the PFA Team of the Year. After five years with Wolves, he signed for Birmingham City in 2022.

Ruddy made his senior debut for England in 2012 in a friendly against Italy.

Association methods in protein–protein interaction (PPI) prediction seek to identify distinguishing features between interacting and non-interacting pairs by analyzing sequence-signatures and interaction site properties. A classifier is trained using association rule mining (ARM), which generates rules that relate these features to PPI types. Specifically, association methods examine sequence motifs and their evolutionary variations to capture binding specificity. Domain interactions are evaluated using a log-odds score, calculated as log(Pij / (Pi * Pj)), where Pij is the probability of interaction between domains, and Pi and Pj are the individual probabilities of each domain. Predicted domain interactions are characterized by their association rules, but a downside is the limited coverage of crystallized protein complexes, which may hinder the analysis of transient interactions.

The symphony has several distinct features. The first movement, written in Sonata-Allegro form, establishes a contrast between an agitated and forte opening theme in C minor, and a lyrical and piano second theme in the relative major (E♭). Greeny is renowned for its distinctive, versatile tone, characterized by a bright yet warm sound with exceptional sustain, as heard in Peter Green’s playing on tracks like *"The Super-Natural."* The guitar is equipped with "Greenybuckers," modified humbuckers featuring a neck pickup rewound with heavy formvar wire—typically found in Strat pickups—after an early breakdown. Green initially sought to emulate Eric Clapton’s blues tone but developed his own signature style, marked by clean, cutting timbres and dynamic subtlety. A critical quirk arose when the neck pickup was reinstalled backward, with its alnico magnet flipped—possibly an aftermarket mod—resulting in an out-of-phase middle position tone, described as "nasally" and unique. Kirk Hammett praised Greeny as "an anomaly" for its singular sound and heavily weathered history, including a severe accident where the neck broke into multiple pieces (reportedly 25). This combination of modifications and damage contributes to its legendary status.

By the early 1930s attitudes towards the policy of Ukrainization had changed within the Soviet leadership. In 1933 Stalin declared that local nationalism was the main threat to Soviet unity. Consequently, many changes introduced during the Ukrainization period were reversed: Russian language schools, libraries and newspapers were restored and even increased in number. In the early 1930s, the Ukrainian SSR ceded territories to Romania and Poland, while thousands of ethnic Ukrainians were deported to Siberia and Central Asia. Many Ukrainian-majority villages were eliminated during the Holodomor. Remaining Ukrainians faced Russification, including the renaming of towns and the closure of Ukrainian-language institutions. Parents could choose between Russian- and Ukrainian-language education, with Russian becoming the primary language of instruction in chosen schools.

McMansions are typically built on undeveloped land that is razed by bulldozer for the development of a subdivision. For instance, "Oak Ridge" or "Laurel Hill" refer to native trees or shrubs whose numbers may have been sharply reduced by the construction work.

Celia's trial took place at a time when slavery was an extremely contentious issue in America, and the verdict had important implications for the legal status of enslaved persons, particularly black women. At that time, slaveowners were allowed to rape, when the legal status of slaves as property offered few protections or privileges of law. Legally, Celia was only seen as a human subject when she was being punished. As Saidiya Hartman states, "As Missouri v. Celia demonstrated, the enslaved could neither give nor refuse consent, nor offer reasonable resistance, yet they were criminally responsible and liable. The slave was recognized as a reasoning subject, who possessed intent and rationality, solely in the context of criminal liability."

Chenopodium fremontii is a species of flowering plant in the family Amaranthaceae known by the common name Frémont's goosefoot. Both the species' specific epithet, and the common name derive from the 19th century western pioneer John C. Frémont.

It is native to much of the western half of North America from Canada through California to Mexico. It grows in many types of habitat from open desert, to shady forest, at 700–3,100 metres (2,300–10,200 ft).

There are currently two products approved as maintenance therapy following treatment of inoperable locoregional Stage III NSCLC with induction chemotherapy: Tarceva (erlotinib), a targeted small molecule from Genentech, a member of the Roche Group, and Alimta (pemetrexed), a chemotherapeutic from Eli Lilly and Company. Tecemotide has not been tested in combination with or in comparison to these products. It is possible that other existing or new agents will be approved for this indication. Celanese Corporation, formerly known as Hoechst Celanese, is an American technology and specialty materials company headquartered in Irving, Texas. A Fortune 500 corporation, the company is the world’s leading producer of acetic acid, producing about 1.95 million tonnes per year, representing approximately 25% of global production. Celanese is also the world's largest producer of vinyl acetate monomer (VAM).

Celanese operates 25 production plants and six research centers in 11 countries, mainly in North America, Europe, and Asia. The company owns and operates the world's three largest acetic acid plants: one in the Clear Lake area of Pasadena, Texas, one on Jurong Island in Singapore, and a third in Nanjing, China.

Saint Melchior is one of the traditional names attributed to one of the Three Magi, or Wise Men, who visited Jesus after his birth, as described in the New Testament. Often depicted as an elderly king from Persia, Melchior is recognized for presenting gold to signify Jesus' kingship. He is venerated in various Christian denominations and commemorated on January 1 and January 6, with his remains housed in the Shrine of the Three Kings at Cologne Cathedral.

On promotion to Rear Admiral, he became, in 2005, the Ministry of Defence's Director General of Development, Concepts and Doctrine, where he was responsible for the doctrinal and conceptual development of the UK Armed Forces out to 2025. Despite fierce opposition from some in the British Army, they were eventually adopted as part of the 'Petraeus Plan' for Iraq. He was also the author of the DCDC Global Strategic Trends Programme 2007-36, which, among other assessments that were subsequently confirmed by events, foresaw the collapse of the US financial system, the re-emergence of piracy at sea and the Russian intervention in Georgia. Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani (1922–1977) was the Emir of Qatar from 1960 to 1972. He ascended to the throne after his father, Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Thani, abdicated. Ahmad oversaw Qatar's independence from Britain in 1971 and implemented some economic reforms, including establishing key government departments. However, he was deposed in a bloodless coup in 1972 by his cousin, Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani. Ahmad lived in exile in Dubai until his death in London in 1977, after which he was buried in Al Rayyan Cemetery in Qatar.

Walhalla-orden was a secret society founded in the early part of 1783 in the Sveaborg (today, in Finnish: Suomenlinna) fortress outside Helsinki, Finland by Johan Anders Jägerhorn along with Gustaf Adolf Reuterholm. The 17th Arizona State Legislature, consisting of the Arizona State Senate and the Arizona House of Representatives, was constituted in Phoenix from January 1, 1945 to December 31, 1946, during the third of Sidney Preston Osborn's four consecutive terms as Governor of Arizona. The number of senators and house members remained constant at 19 and 58, respectively. The Democrats controlled one hundred percent of the senate. while the Republicans gained a single house seat.

Dharmambal founded the Manavar Mandram ("Student Forum") to bring education in Tamil to the youth of Chennai. Dharmambal conducted the 'Elavu Varam' agitation to secure equal pay for Tamil teachers and supported widow remarriage, inter-caste marriages, and women's education.

The polyvagal theory is another way to describe the pathways in the autonomic nervous system that mediate HRV. The polyvagal theory highlights three main ordinal processes, inactive response to an environmental threat, the active response to an environmental threat, and the fluctuation between the connect and disconnect to an environmental threat. This theory, like others, decomposes heart rate variability based on frequency domain characteristics. However, it places more emphasis on respiratory sinus arrhythmia and its transmission by a hypothesized neural pathway distinct from other components of HRV. There is anatomic and physiological evidence for a polyvagal control of the heart.

The rare spotted handfish (Brachionichthys hirsutus), whose only habitat is in the Derwent estuary and surrounds, was the first marine fish to be listed as critically endangered in the IUCN Red List, in 1996. The fish is threatened by the Northern Pacific seastar's invasion into southern Australian waters. The Northern Pacific seastar (Asterias amurensis), now firmly established in the Derwent, preys on not only the fish eggs, but also on the sea squirts (ascidians) that help to form the substrate that the fish spawn on.

Gdańsk Pomerania or Eastern Pomerania (Polish: Pomorze Wschodnie; Kashubian: Pòrénkòwô Pòmòrskô) is a geographical region today in northern Poland, covering the eastern part of the Pomeranian Voivodeship. This area, known historically as Pomerelia, Słupsk, Chełmno Land, Lubawa, Michałów Land, and Pomesania, was part of West Prussia and Royal Prussia before 1945. Post-World War II, much of the area became part of the new Second Polish Republic, and after World War II, the Soviet Union gave Poland the remainder of the area, including the city of Gdańsk (Danzig). Current designations reflect these changes, with parts of Farther Pomerania and a small part of Hither Pomerania (Vorpommern) also ceded to Poland as part of the so-called Recovered Territories from Germany.

According to a study using U.S. linked birth and infant death cohort files from 1995 to 2000, infants born to Mexican immigrant women had a 10% lower mortality rate in the first week of life compared to non-Hispanic, white U.S.-born women. This finding supports the epidemiologic paradox, where immigrants often have better health outcomes than the native-born population. Refugees, particularly Palestine refugees, also exhibit low infant and childhood mortality rates despite facing significant socioeconomic hardships. In Gaza, infant mortality rates are 4.4 times higher than in Israel, indicating similar neonatal issues. Key causes include inadequate neonatal care, with leading causes being preterm birth, low birth weight, and infections. These trends are comparable to those observed among rural and indigenous populations in other regions.

A fish flake is a traditional structure used for drying cod, consisting of a horizontal framework of small poles supported by upright poles. Located near fishing stages, flakes were either permanent or temporary (hand flakes) and essential for preserving fish through natural drying. First noted in the 1770s, these structures were widely used until the mid-20th century, when cold storage and freezers reduced their necessity. The drying process involved spreading salted cod on the flakes, alternating sides to expose them to sunlight, and storing them in fish stores until market readiness.

For ITTF world title events, a player is eligible to play for his association by registering with the ITTF. If the player chooses to play for a new association, he shall register with the ITTF, through the new association. The player will be eligible to play for the new association after three, five, seven years after the date of registration, if the player is under the age of 15, 18, 21 respectively. The player will be eligible to play for the new association after nine years if the player is at least 21 years old.

Indian surrogates became increasingly popular amongst intended parents in industrialized nations because of the relatively low costs and easy access offered by Indian surrogacy agencies. Game reviewers Hartley and Pattie Lesser complimented the game in their "The Role of Computers" column in Dragon #122 (1987), calling it "the finest arcade/adventure game ever designed for the Macintosh computer — as a matter of fact, for any computer!" and stating, "The graphics and animation are quite literally stunning!". The shiner lives in small to medium sized freshwater streams with minimal gradient and substrate ranging from sand to bedrock. The shiner prefers to live in deep pools that are downstream from water willows. The fish mainly lives in the Black Warrior River system in Alabama. Populations of Lythrurus alegnotus co-exist with populations of Lythrurus bellus in Lost Creek in Walker County, Alabama, the North River, and in Yellow Creek and Hurricane Creek in Tuscaloosa County, Alabama.
Medical programs in Korea used to be direct-entry programs, taking 6 years to complete. Most universities are now transitioning to a 4+4 year system. There are 41 medical schools in South Korea.

Of all homicide victims age 21 and younger in Boston from 1990 to 1994, 75% had a prior criminal record. In Philadelphia, the percentage of gun homicide victims with prior criminal records increased from 73% in 1985 to 93% in 1996.

Christine Maggiore, an HIV-positive activist and AIDS denialist, died on December 27, 2008, at the age of 52. Initially, officials at the Los Angeles County coroner's office reported she had been treated for pneumonia in the last six months. A doctor noted that a "detox cleansing" regimen might have weakened her, making her susceptible to pneumonia. AIDS denialists argued that her death was not due to AIDS, expecting to spin the results to support their claims. The diagnosis of DFSP-FS depends on finding a dermal tumor with characteristic areas consisting of spindle-shaped cells with atypical, vesicular nuclei aligned in smooth muscle-like bundled or herringbone patterns. The spindle-shaped cells are often rapidly growing as indicated by their relatively high mitotic index. These sites form from 5% to 100% of the tumor with any remaining sites consisting of DFSP lesions (i.e. bland, uniform, relatively slowly proliferating spindle-shaped tumor cells arranged in a characteristic cartwheel or whorled pattern). The diagnosis of DFSP-FS is strongly supported by finding that the tumors' cells express the COL1A1-PDGFB fusion gene; a finding that these tumor cells do not express or only weakly express CD34 protein strongly indicates that it is a DFSP-FS rather than a DFSP tumor.

The main feature of Rakovník’s urban monument zone, located in the centre of the town, is the 400 metre-long Hus Square, where the 16th-century town hall with a late Baroque façade is located. The interior of the town hall is decorated with a ceiling fresco depicting the town of Rakovník as it was 250 years ago. The eastern part of the square is dominated by the high Gothic Decanal Church of St. Bartholomew, a 14th-century structure.

George Gordon began his Australian career in 1871 when he was commissioned by the Victorian government to report on the public waterworks. Although his initial appointment was canceled, the Duffy government reopened negotiations in October 1871, and Gordon arrived in Melbourne on 5 May 1872 to assume the role of chief engineer of the Board of Lands and Works. He continued the work of engineer Joseph Brady on the Coliban Water Supply for Bendigo and Mount Alexander, a critical project for the region's goldfields. In 1873, Gordon achieved a significant milestone by designing and constructing the Lower Stony Creek concrete dam for the Geelong water supply, a project of scientific importance. On 2 February 1875, he was appointed chief engineer of the Victorian Water Supply Department, overseeing the state's water infrastructure. However, his tenure ended abruptly on 9 January 1878, known as Black Wednesday, when he was dismissed during a government purge. Despite raising his dismissal in parliament and petitioning the governor and the Queen for reinstatement and compensation, his efforts were unsuccessful. Gordon's contributions to Victoria's water supply systems remain a testament to his expertise as a hydraulic engineer.

On 20 July 2008, during a party convention, Storace resigned from party leadership, opening way for a national congress and a leadership election. This election would also decide the party's electoral strategy, choosing either to continue an independent path or to join The People of Freedom (PdL) of Silvio Berlusconi. Storace favoured the first option, while Santanchè favoured an alliance with Berlusconi. Storace would continue to be party secretary at least until the November congress and after if party members decided so.

Pinecone Burke Provincial Park is a provincial park in British Columbia, Canada. It extends from the southwest corner of Garibaldi Provincial Park, west of Pitt Lake and Pitt River to include Burke Mountain in the City of Coquitlam. Most of the park is unserviced wilderness with very rough trails such as the Fools Gold Trail in the Boise Valley. There is canoe access to Widgeon Slough and from there hiking trails that lead to Widgeon Lake, and a network of old logging roads, hiking and mountain biking trails on Burke Mountain. The parks boundaries were established in 1995 by the provincial government and covers 38,000 hectares. Parts of the parks boundaries are located in the Katzie First Nation, Kwikwetlem First Nation, and Stó:lō First Nations territories. The parks diverse ecosystems supports several different species on land and in its inlet. There are 6 species that have been labeled as sensitive or vulnerable that live in the park. Before the parks protected status the area was logged and mined. It also was used for recreational purposes such as hiking and rock climbing. Visitors can use the park for hiking, camping, back country skiing and snowshoeing.

The C. A. Holliday Transfer Facility is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice (TDCJ) transfer unit for male inmates located in Huntsville, Texas. Named for C. A. Holliday, an African-American community activist and pastor, the facility opened in January 1994. The prison was the result of an effort led by Richard Watkins, a senior African-American prison warden, who advocated for a prison to be named in Holliday's honor. The facility has a capacity of around 2,000 beds.

The active site of MetAP2 features a unique structural motif typical of metalloenzymes, characterized by the coordination of two metal ions, usually cobalt or manganese. In human MetAP2 (PDB code: 1B6A), the metal ions are stabilized by bridging carboxylate ligands, specifically aspartate and glutamate residues, along with a bidentate histidine residue that binds one of the metal ions. The other metal ion is coordinated by additional residues, including another histidine. The two bridging carboxylates are crucial for the enzyme's catalytic function, facilitating the cleavage of N-terminal methionine residues from nascent polypeptides.

The most commonly spoken language in Switzerland is German. German is a gendered language. This has concerned some language activists due to the fact that many important societal positions such as judge and professor possess the gender of male and are often referred to as he/him. Activists worry that the gendering of those words discourages women from entering those fields. This facet of the German language is particularly important in Switzerland because it was historically used as a justification to restrict women's right to vote and pass the bar.

An eruption column is a vertical plume of volcanic ash and tephra formed during explosive volcanic activity, where volatile materials in rising magma are disrupted. These columns can reach heights of several kilometers, propelled by strong convection currents, and may be transient or sustained depending on the nature of the eruption. The height of an eruption column is influenced by intrinsic factors such as vent diameter and gas content, as well as extrinsic factors like wind and atmospheric temperature gradients. Eruption columns can inject particulates into the stratosphere, potentially causing global temperature changes, as seen after the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption. Additionally, column collapse can lead to dangerous pyroclastic flows. Eruption columns pose significant hazards to aviation, prompting the establishment of monitoring networks to mitigate risks associated with volcanic ash.

Kuressaare is a town located on Saaremaa Island in Estonia, with a population of 12,698 as of 2021. First documented in 1154, it developed around the medieval Kuressaare Castle, which now houses the Saaremaa Regional Museum. Historically known as Arensburg, the town has undergone various name changes and governance, including periods under Danish, Swedish, and Russian control. Kuressaare is recognized for its cultural events, such as the Saaremaa Opera Days, and serves as a popular seaside resort.

Nauru has used its position as a member of the UN to gain financial support from both the Republic of China (ROC) and the People's Republic of China (PRC) by changing its position on the political status of Taiwan. During 2002, Nauru signed an agreement to establish diplomatic relations with the PRC on 21 July. This move followed mainland China's promise to provide more than US$60 million in aid. In response, the ROC severed diplomatic relations with Nauru two days later. Nauru later re-established links with the ROC on 14 May 2005, and diplomatic ties with the PRC were officially severed on 31 May 2005; however, the PRC continues to maintain a diplomatic presence in the island nation.

Pivotal (19 January 1993 – 19 November 2021) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and influential sire. Bred and owned by Cheveley Park Stud, he was trained by Sir Mark Prescott and had a racing record of six starts, including major wins in the 1996 King's Stand Stakes and Nunthorpe Stakes. Retired to stud in 1997, Pivotal became a highly successful breeding stallion, siring numerous major winners. He was retired from stud duties in 2021 and died later that year.

In his original description, Reusch regarded the strandflat as originating from marine abrasion prior to glaciation, but adding that some levelling could have been caused by non-marine erosion. In his view, the formation of the strandflat preceded the fjords of Norway. "Years later, in 1919, Hans Ahlmann proposed that the strandflat formed by erosion on land towards a base level. In the mid-20th century, W. Evers argued it was a low-erosion surface formed on land as part of a stepped sequence, termed piedmonttreppen, which included Paleic surfaces. Olaf Holtedahl refuted this, noting the surfaces' positions did not align with a piedmonttreppen."

*Madden NFL 25* is a 2013 sports video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by EA Sports as part of the *Madden NFL* series. Released for PlayStation 3, Xbox 360, iOS, and Android on August 27, 2013, it later launched as a title for PlayStation 4 and Xbox One. The game introduced the Ignite engine for next-gen consoles, offering enhanced graphics, real-time physics, and improved gameplay mechanics, including more realistic player movements and environmental interactions. It also marked the return of team relocation in the Connected Franchise mode.

Barry Sanders, a Hall of Fame running back, was featured on the cover for seventh-generation consoles, while Adrian Peterson appeared on the next-gen versions. The game received mostly positive reviews for its gameplay improvements but faced criticism for commentary and repetitive elements. Despite mixed reception for its mobile versions, *Madden NFL 25* sold over 3 million copies.

TVR Cultural is a Romanian television channel dedicated to culture and the arts, originally launched in 2002 by Televiziunea Română. It ceased broadcasting in 2012 due to financial difficulties but was relaunched in 2022, offering programming in both SD and HD formats.

The term polka possibly comes from the Czech word "půlka" ("half"), referring to the short half-steps featured in the dance. Czech cultural historian and ethnographer Čeněk Zíbrt, who wrote in detail about the origin of the dance, in his book, Jak se kdy v Čechách tancovalo cites an opinion of František Doucha (1840, Květy, p. 400) that "polka" was supposed to mean "dance in half" ("tanec na polo"), both referring to the half-tempo and the half-jump step of the dance. Zíbrt also ironically dismisses the etymology suggested by A. Fähnrich (in Ein etymologisches Taschenbuch, Jiein, 1846) that "polka" comes from the Czech word "pole" ("field").

Sullivan & Cromwell was founded in 1879 by Algernon Sydney Sullivan and William Nelson Cromwell. The firm advised J.P. Morgan on the creation of Edison General Electric in 1882. Key players under Sullivan & Cromwell's guidance formed U.S. Steel in 1901. William Nelson Cromwell developed the concept of a holding company, which he persuaded New Jersey to include in its state law to lower fees and make it possible for one corporation to own stock in another. This facilitated the formation of holding companies, replacing the trusts that had been broken up. During the volatile decades before modern bankruptcy laws, the firm specialized in rescuing companies and merging them into larger units. Sullivan & Cromwell pioneered the plan to reorganize insolvent companies, often referred to as a "workout."

It was in Mecca, in the nearby mountain cave of Hira, that, according to Islamic tradition, Mohammad is said to have begun receiving divine revelations from God through the Angel Gabriel in 610 AD, and began to preach his form of Abrahamic monotheism against Meccan paganism. After enduring persecution from the pagan tribes for 13 years, Muhammad emigrated (see Hijra) in 622 with his companions to Yathrib (later called Madinah). The conflict between the Quraysh and the Muslims, however, continued: the two fought in the Battle of Badr, where the Muslims defeated the Quraysh army outside Madinah; while the Meccans overcame the Muslims at the Battle of Uhud.

Wafa Mustafa (born in Masyaf, Syria) is a Syrian journalist and activist known for her advocacy for detainees and missing persons in Syria, including her father, Ali Mustafa, who was forcibly disappeared in 2013. After participating in early anti-government protests, she was arrested in 2011 and later fled to Turkey before settling in Germany. She graduated from Bard College Berlin in 2020. Mustafa has campaigned internationally, including at the UN Security Council and the Al Khatib trial in Germany, where she highlighted human rights abuses by the Syrian regime. She co-founded *Families for Freedom*, a movement demanding justice for Syria's disappeared.

In literary theory, polyphony is a narrative structure where multiple voices and perspectives are presented without a single, dominant authorial voice imposing a unified view. This concept, developed by Mikhail Bakhtin, contrasts with monologic narratives, where the author's ideology controls the work and characters' ideas are either affirmed or repudiated.

In a polyphonic work, characters possess their own "voice-ideas," an artistic fusion of personality and idea, allowing them to "signify directly." These voices are "unmerged" and cannot be contained within a single consciousness, even when they agree. The author relinquishes control, confronting characters as equals and participating in a "great dialogue" where the outcome is not predetermined. This creates an "open-ended dialogue" that reflects the true, lived nature of human existence, where unforeseen possibilities arise from the interaction of autonomous consciousnesses. Bakhtin argues that polyphony is not merely an artistic technique but a reflection of a broader cultural shift away from monologic thinking.

Mutational meltdown is a process in population genetics where the accumulation of deleterious mutations in a small population leads to a decline in population size and growth rate, ultimately resulting in extinction. This occurs as harmful mutations become fixed in the population, reducing fitness and increasing mortality rates. The process is particularly rapid in small populations due to genetic drift and the shorter time required for mutations to become fixed.

The effects of mutational meltdown differ between asexual and sexual populations. Asexual populations are more vulnerable due to the lack of genetic recombination, which allows mutations to accumulate more quickly. In contrast, sexual populations benefit from genetic diversity and recombination, which can slow or prevent mutational meltdown. However, under certain conditions, such as low birth rates or population bottlenecks, even large sexually reproducing populations can experience mutational meltdown.

Capacitor discharge (CD) stud welding differs from drawn arc stud welding primarily in its suitability for thinner base materials and smaller-diameter studs, as well as its faster weld times, typically completed in 0.01 seconds. Unlike drawn arc welding, CD stud welding does not require flux or shielding gas, simplifying the process and reducing costs. The rapid discharge of electrical energy creates a precise arc, minimizing heat buildup and preventing warping, discoloration, or reverse side marking, making it ideal for thin metals as light as 0.020" (0.5mm). CD stud welding is commonly used in applications requiring clean, aesthetically pleasing welds, such as automotive, electronics, and lightweight manufacturing. In contrast, drawn arc stud welding is better suited for larger fasteners and thicker, rougher base materials in heavy-duty construction and fabrication.

The Ural Society of Natural Science Lovers, despite its contributions, faced decline after the formation of the Soviet Union. Consequently, several prominent members were repressed, and the Society was forcibly closed in 1929.

Molding sand, also known as foundry sand, is a sand that when moistened and compressed or oiled or heated tends to pack well and hold its shape. It is used in the process of sand casting for preparing the mould cavity.

As president of both the International Federation for Peace and Sustainable Development (IFPSD) and the US Federation For Middle East Peace (USFMEP), Sally Kader has actively promoted youth empowerment through various initiatives. She has promoted Montessori Model United Nations events and inter-faith dialogue events involving youth. Kader has hosted several panel discussions on human trafficking. As a member of the Commission on the Status of Women (CIS), Kader supports the Virtual Global Task Force, which is significant for its work on gender equality and access to justice.

", found large popular appeal and worldwide chart success, other later specific attempts of Drummond and Cauty to top the charts were less successful. In 1989, The KLF released "Kylie Said to Jason," which failed to chart in the UK Top 100. A decade later, Cauty collaborated with Guy Pratt on the novelty project Solid Gold Chartbusters, aiming for a Christmas 1999 hit, but their single "I Wanna 1-2-1 With You" missed the UK Top 40. In 2005, the mash-up project *American Edit* featured "Doctor Who On Holiday," combining "Doctorin' the Tardis" with Green Day's "Holiday," showcasing the enduring influence of The KLF's work.

Oxalis latifolia is a species of flowering plant in the woodsorrel family known by the common names garden pink-sorrel and broadleaf woodsorrel. It is native to Mexico and parts of Central and South America.

The Peerage Act 1963 allows the holder of an hereditary peerage to disclaim their title for life. There is no such provision for life peers. The Coalition Government's draft proposal for Lords reform in 2011 "provides that a person who holds a life peerage may at any time disclaim that peerage by writing to the Lord Chancellor. The person [and their spouse and children] will be divested of all rights and interests attaching to [that] peerage." This proposal did not become law. In 2014 under the House of Lords Reform Act it became possible for peers to resign from the House of Lords (without disclaiming the peerage).

Augustus Jones (c. 1757–1836) was a prominent land surveyor and farmer who played a crucial role in shaping the geography and politics of southern Ontario. Born in the Hudson River Valley, Jones trained as a surveyor in New York City before fleeing to Upper Canada with his family in the 1780s due to his loyalty to the British Crown. He served as the Deputy Surveyor General for the Nassau District (Home District) from 1789 to 1799, surveying numerous townships and roads, including the famous Jones Baseline and the initial route of Yonge Street. Jones also held positions as a magistrate and militia captain, and was known for his fluency in indigenous languages and his strong relationships with local Native communities. After retiring from surveying in 1800, he became a farmer and moved to Brantford in 1817. He is remembered for his extensive contributions to the development of Ontario, and a statue honoring his work was unveiled in Stoney Creek in 2005.

In their first home game of the 1959 season, the Alabama Crimson Tide faced the Chattanooga Mocs under cold conditions with a winter storm affecting attendance. Alabama won decisively with a final score of 49-0. Robert Skelton scored the first touchdown on a 1-yard run, followed by Pat Trammell, who rushed for 110 yards on 15 carries. With this victory, Alabama improved its all-time record against Chattanooga to 13-0.

Deccani Architecture refers to the architectural styles developed during the Deccan sultanate period. The Deccan sultanates were five dynasties that ruled late medieval kingdoms, namely, Bijapur, Golkonda, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, and Berar in south-western India. The Deccan sultanates were located on the Deccan Plateau. Their architecture was a regional variant of Indo-Islamic architecture, heavily influenced by the styles of the Delhi Sultanate and later Mughal architecture, but sometimes also directly from Persia and Central Asia.

The rulers of five Deccan sultanates had a number of cultural contributions to their credit in the fields of art, music, literature and architecture. Deccan sultanates have constructed many grand and impregnable forts. Bidar and Golconda forts are classic example of military planning of Deccan sultanates. Apart from forts, they have constructed many tombs, mosques and madrasas. Gol Gumbaz (tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah), was the second largest dome in the world.

The Katkari were at one time a forest people living in the Western Ghats of Maharashtra, with a special relationship to forest creatures such as the tiger or ‘waghmare’, (wagh = tiger, mare = slayer; so tiger slayer) a common Katkari surname. The name Katkari is derived from a forest-based activity – the making and sale of catechu (katha) from the khair tree (Acacia catechu). Weling (1934, 2), drawing on census data from 1901, notes that the Katkari were ‘thickly scattered’ in small communities throughout the hill ranges and forests of Raigad and Thane districts in the present day state of Maharashtra. Some also lived in hill areas in the southern part of the current state of Gujarat, and in the forests of what are now Nashik, Pune and Dhule districts.

The Book Division of the William L. Clements Library encompasses a vast array of subjects beyond William L. Clements’ original Americana collection, including Native American history, territoriality, and social issues such as assimilation and Indian-settler interactions. The collection highlights 18th and 19th-century American history, with a focus on the Americas. Contributions from collectors like James S. Schoff, who donated Civil War unit and campaign histories, and James V. Medler, who enriched the library with materials on women’s history, have significantly shaped its holdings. The library also preserves works on geography, land use, and women’s roles in history, offering a comprehensive resource for understanding the social and cultural dynamics of the region.

Matthew Seers (born 28 June 1974) is a former Australian rugby league player who played as a fullback, centre, and five-eighth. He had a notable career with Norths Devils from 1993 to 1999, representing Country Origin and New South Wales. Seers also played for Wests Tigers, Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, and Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks. He faced legal troubles in 2015 for drug offenses.

In addition to the association matrix, the activities matrix can be used to produce actionable information, which has practical value and use to law-enforcement. The activities matrix, as the term might imply, centers on the actions and activities of people with respect to locations. Whereas the association matrix focuses on the relationships between people, organizations, and/or properties. The distinction between these two types of matrices, while minor, is nonetheless significant in terms of the output of the analysis completed or rendered.

JoBeth Williams starred alongside Gene Wilder and Richard Pryor in the 1980 prison comedy "Stir Crazy," where she played Meredith. In "The Dogs of War" (1980), she co-starred with Christopher Walken, Colin Blakely, and others. Williams portrayed Diane Freeling in the horror classic "Poltergeist" (1982) and reprised the role in its 1986 sequel, "Poltergeist II: The Other Side." In 1983, she joined an ensemble cast in "The Big Chill." Williams won the 1985 Best Actress Award from the Kansas City Film Critics Circle for her performance in "Baby M" (1988). In "Teachers" (1984), she starred with John Heard, and in 1986, she appeared alongside Jon Voight in "Desert Bloom."
Mouse-Warming is a 1952 Warner Bros. Looney Tunes short directed by Chuck Jones. The cartoon was released on September 8, 1952 and stars Claude Cat in his final solo cartoon.

Studies have demonstrated that DCPA acts as a chemical disruptor by interfering with microtubule formation in exposed cells. This interference results in abnormal cell division. "Abnormal microtubules compromise cell-wall formation, leading to defective mitotic division. Unlike other mitotic inhibitors, DCPA's limited systemic movement restricts its effects. It often produces stunted cells and kills plants by inhibiting spindle fiber alignment during mitosis."

Peggy Dern, born Erolie Pearl Gaddis, was a prolific author who wrote under numerous pseudonyms, reportedly close to 300 books. Most of her works were published under her married name, Peggy Dern, or as Peggy Gaddis. Frequent pseudonyms included Gerald Foster and Perry Lindsay, among others. She also used other pen names to diversify her output, particularly in romance and nurse-themed stories.

Bader Philanthropies Inc. is a Milwaukee, Wisconsin based foundation that consists of funds from the Helen Daniels Bader Fund and the Isabel and Alfred Bader Fund. It pledges to give away $14 million annually. The organization centers on the health of older adults and improving lives of low-income Milwaukeeans and Jewish education in Milwaukee.

Several improvements were made prior to the Americans' 1886 season. The first scoreboard was a blackboard on which scores were displayed by writing figures in chalk. In September 1885, Summer Street (Fifth Avenue) was raised, which necessitated raising the adjacent fence to prevent onlookers. An additional row of boards was placed atop the Jackson Street fence, and a second fence was erected around the entire park inside the existing fence to further prevent unpaid viewing of games over or through the fence. The first parts of the book are devoted to a critical analysis of early works on a supposed biologically inherited basis for intelligence, such as craniometry, the measurement of skull volume and its relation to intellectual faculties. Gould argues that much of this research was based more on prejudice than scientific rigor, demonstrating how in several occasions researchers such as Samuel Morton, Louis Agassiz, and Paul Broca committed the fallacy of using their expected conclusions as part of their reasoning. The book contains a complete re-working of original data for one of these studies, showing that the original results were based biases and manipulations, mostly by selection of data. When these biases are accounted for, the original hypothesis—an ordering in skull size ranging from Blacks through Mongols to Whites—is not supported in any way by the data.

Sally Kader, President of the International Federation for Peace and Sustainable Development (IFPSD), plays a pivotal role in promoting children's rights and youth empowerment. She has supported the Montessori Model United Nations conferences for youth empowerment since 2017. In 2019, she organized an interfaith dialogue event for 30 imams and rabbis from 13 European countries. Kader also hosted several panel discussions on human trafficking. As a member of the Council on International Society (CIS), she supports the Virtual Global Task Force, which aims to enhance access to justice and gender equality globally.

Purcell helped to defend the town during the Siege of Limerick. At the surrender on 27 October 1651 he was one of the 22 exempted by name from pardon. He hid in the pest house and was arrested there together with the Terence O'Brien, bishop of Emly. At the court-marshal Purcell fell on his knees and begged for his life. At his hanging he was held up by two of Ireton's musqueteers. His head was affixed on St John's Gate, the southern gate of Limerick's Irishtown.

SB 94 was passed in June 2019 in the final days of the legislative session. US$6 million was allocated as part of the bill to fund reproductive assistance measures in the state through family planning grants. The grants funded preventive services like mobile body scans but excluded abortion. Funds targeted low-income individuals in Nevada, prioritizing public charities under IRS 501(c)(3). Donor-advised funds could support eligible nonprofits, but illegal medical procedures remained ineligible.

Rametti and colleagues used diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) to compare 18 homosexual male-to-female transsexual individuals with 19 heterosexual males and 19 heterosexual females. The homosexual transsexuals showed differences in multiple brain areas, including the superior longitudinal fasciculus, the right anterior cingulum, the right forceps minor, and the right corticospinal tract, compared to the control groups. The study authors noted these differences but did not draw conclusions about the nature or significance of these findings.

The University of Florida clinical toxicology distance education program is an online graduate program offered by the UF College of Pharmacy. It includes a Master of Science (MS) in Clinical Toxicology, a 32-credit degree for health professionals, and a 15-credit graduate certificate in clinical toxicology. Both programs focus on medical and toxicological principles related to toxicants, drug analysis, and overdose treatment. The MS program is designed for poison control specialists, pharmacists, nurses, physicians, and emergency responders, while the certificate provides foundational knowledge for similar professionals. The program is entirely online, allowing working professionals to advance their expertise in clinical toxicology.

In 2021, Huhtamaki had 18,400 employees and 79 production facilities, located in countries such as Poland, Russia, Ukraine, Spain, Ireland, the United Kingdom, Turkey, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, India, China, Malaysia, Australia and the United States. One of the facilities is located in the Nordic countries. The Hämeenlinna factory produces, for example, cardboard cups, ice-cream packaging, salad boxes and disposable plates. The Nordic countries employ approximately 300 people. Huhtamäki's North American operations are based within the Kansas City Metropolitan Area in De Soto, Kansas.
